original_post.author: Scott Northwolf
original_post.title: 🚨Say hello to the gang ❗
original_post.body: Welcome, brother!  Let's get started by introducing yourself.  Answer the following questions on your post:  1. Can you briefly share from A to Z how you got into self-improvement?  2. What was the pain that got you into self-improvement?  3. How did you get into self-improvement coaching/consulting?  4. How long have you've been a self-improvement coach?  5. At what stage of your journey are you? (no shame if you're just getting started, we all got to start somewhere).

comment.author: Devon Hoyt
comment.content: What’s up everyone 👋 glad to be here.My name is Devon.1. From A to Z — how I got into self-improvement:Life kept handing me wake-up calls. Health scares, financial stress, family responsibilities, and realizing that nobody was coming to save me. I started learning out of necessity—how systems work, how habits work, how mindset works—because staying the same wasn’t an option anymore.2. The pain that pushed me:Feeling stuck while knowing I had people depending on me. That mix of frustration, fear, and responsibility is a powerful teacher. I didn’t want to just survive—I wanted to become someone my kids could look up to.3. Coaching / consulting path:Not a formal coach yet. I’m more in the “learn it, apply it, share it” phase. I help people by breaking things down simply, sharing what I’ve tested in real life, and being honest about what works and what doesn’t.4. How long I’ve been in self-improvement:Several years of trial, error, setbacks, and course corrections. Still learning every day.5. Current stage of my journey:Actively building. Still sharpening habits, tightening discipline, and leveling up consistency. Not finished—just committed.Looking forward to learning from everyone here and contributing where I can. 👊

replies.author: Scott Northwolf
replies.content: @Devon Hoyt epic, Devon! Thank you for sharing. Only when the pain of staying the same outweights the pain of facing the resistance and making a change is when we'll finally make a change. I heard from great men that the moment in which the truly became men was the very moment in which they realized nobody was coming to save them and they had to figure things out by themselves, it has been my experience as well.I'm really glad to have you here and looking forward to connect.

comment.author: Andrea Piras
comment.content: I got into self-improvement in the summer of 2022, almost 4 years ago. I did it because I had very few friends and life wasn't going as I wanted, so I decided to start meditating, journaling, exercising at home and various stuff, and I felt great. From that point I understood that it was lifestyle rather than a period of my life, so I have never stopped since then.I got into self-improvement coaching thanks to the Charlie Morgan's course (still watching it).I couldn't call myself a coach yet, since I haven't had any client yet, but I started following che course about a month ago.Right now I'm in a stage where I'm focusing on fixing my relationships and skills, and looking back I see that I have made a huge progress.

replies.author: Scott Northwolf
replies.content: @Andrea Piras you certainly have made insane progress, brother. I’m very proud of you. Stay on course.

comment.author: Miguel Ariza Lora
comment.content: What's up gang,My name is Miguel, 25 years old and Spanish-Dutch currently living in Thailand.My journey in self-improvement began around 15 years old when I was overweight. I really wanted to be more like the others my age. Luckily this is when YouTube began to take off and people starting sharing a lot of value around fitness and nutrition. Trial and error got me to losing 20kg's eventually, and I've been able to keep most of it off ever since. When I saw putting some work in could give me results, I realized the same thing could be done in other aspects.The following years I became a lot more extroverted, social and confident. Through studying social work and psychology I got into coaching people to build a better life. I was good doing those jobs, and now I've taken it online and moved to Thailand. Felt like a good next step in my self-improvement. Have been coaching people now for 7 years already. Learned a whole lot. About people, training techniques, but probably even more about myself.I just think the self-improvement journey is amazing, and I wish everyone can experience seeing themselves improve into a better version and better life, step by step. Does it ever end? I don't think so. There's always room for improvement. It can feel tiring at times, but I wouldn't want to have it any other way.Can't wait to connect more with all of you guys.- Miguel

original_post.author: Benjamin S.
original_post.title: KPI Tracker
original_post.body: Hey guys, I made this cool KPI Tracker based on what @Scott Northwolf was being teaching and based on his latest post about setting goals.  It has a revenue planner in it, so all you have to do is simply type in your stats based on calls that you've had, and it will tell you exactly how many outreach per day you need in order to achieve your goal.  I made it with Claude AI and you can do the same. I will leave both the file and prompt in case if you want to modify for your own needs.

comment.author: Benjamin S.
comment.content: (edited)Here's the promised prompt(btw if you want to change the theme/vibe, just change it from bali, indonesia to anything you want):Create a simple KPI tracking app with a tropical Bali, Indonesia vibe.Main goal:Build a clean, motivating KPI tracker that tracks only 4 business metrics, calculates them automatically, reverse-engineers the sales process, and adds light gamification.The 4 KPIs to track are:1. SCR = Sales Conversion Rate2. GFR = Good Fit Rate3. SUR = Show Up Rate4. ABR = Appointment Booking RateImportant rule:All KPI values must be treated as decimals internally and in the input fields.Examples:- 30% = 0.30- 50% = 0.50- 75% = 0.75The app should still display the percentage clearly for the user.Example:If SCR = 0.50, also show “50%”--------------------------------------------------PART 1: KPI TRACKER--------------------------------------------------Create a simple section where I can enter raw numbers and the app calculates each KPI automatically.Use these formulas:- SCR = Clients Closed ÷ Sales CallsExample:Sales Calls = 2Clients Closed = 1SCR = 0.50Display = 50%- GFR = Good Fit Calls ÷ Show UpsExample:Show Ups = 10Good Fit Calls = 6GFR = 0.60Display = 60%- SUR = Show Ups ÷ BookingsExample:Bookings = 8Show Ups = 4SUR = 0.50Display = 50%- ABR = Bookings ÷ Outreach VolumeExample:Outreach Volume = 100Bookings = 20ABR = 0.20Display = 20%For each KPI card include:- KPI name- short formula- numerator input- denominator input- decimal result- percentage result- small tooltip/help textMake the tracker very simple and fast to use.--------------------------------------------------PART 2: REVERSE ENGINEERING CALCULATOR--------------------------------------------------The app must include a reverse-engineering planner using this exact formula chain:$ Goal ÷ Client Value = Clients RequiredClients Required ÷ SCR = Good Fit Calls RequiredGood Fit Calls Required ÷ GFR = Show Ups RequiredShow Ups Required ÷ SUR = Bookings RequiredBookings Required ÷ ABR = Outreach Volume RequiredOutreach Volume Required ÷ Days to Achieve Goal = Daily Activity RequiredImportant:- all metrics must be entered as decimals- example: 30% = 0.30- never interpret 30 as 30%- 0.30 is the valid input for 30%Inputs:- Revenue Goal ($)- Client Value ($)- SCR (decimal)- GFR (decimal)- SUR (decimal)- ABR (decimal)- Days to Achieve GoalOutputs:- Clients Required- Good Fit Calls Required- Show Ups Required- Bookings Required- Outreach Volume Required- Daily Activity RequiredRules:- round up all planning outputs where appropriate because in real life you can’t have part of a client or part of a call- update outputs instantly when inputs change- validate against division by zero- show friendly error messages- include helper text reminding the user that KPI inputs are decimals like 0.30--------------------------------------------------PART 3: GAMIFICATION--------------------------------------------------Gamify the app in a motivating but not childish way.Add a reward system with:- XP points- levels- weekly streak- monthly streakXP system ideas:- gain XP when entering data daily- gain XP for hitting or improving KPI benchmarks- gain XP for completing a weekly review- gain XP for maintaining streaksLevel system:Use tropical/Bali-inspired progression names such as:- Traveler- Explorer- Island Builder- Wave Rider- Temple Guardian- Master of the IslandShow:- current level- XP bar- XP needed for next levelStreak system:- weekly streak = number of consecutive weeks with activity/data entry- monthly streak = number of consecutive months with activity/data entry- current streak- best streak- streak reward bonus XPOptional:- small celebration animation when a streak is maintained- badge icons for consistency milestones--------------------------------------------------PART 4: THEME AND DESIGN--------------------------------------------------Theme:Bali, Indonesia tropical vibesVisual direction:- tropical island feeling- clean and relaxing- palm leaves, ocean tones, sunset colors, warm sand colors- modern and minimal, not cluttered- peaceful but motivating- premium-looking, not childish- subtle tropical gradients- cards should feel fresh, airy, and smooth- the UI should feel like productivity on a beautiful islandColor inspiration:- turquoise / ocean blue- sunset orange- soft palm green- warm beige / sand- white or off-white cards if needed- or a darker tropical sunset mode if it looks betterMake sure:- text is highly readable- numbers are clear- dashboard feels calm and satisfying- mobile layout feels just as good as desktop--------------------------------------------------PART 5: DEVICE SUPPORT--------------------------------------------------Important:The app must work well on both:- PC- iPhoneRequirements:- fully responsive design- mobile-first friendly- easy inputs on iPhone- large tap targets- smooth layout on small screensBest option:- cloud sync / cloud sharing across devices so I can use the same tracker on PC and iPhoneIf cloud sync is used:- use a simple solution like Supabase or Firebase- keep setup simple- make sure data persists across devicesIf cloud sync is too complex:- provide the easiest good fallback- but prioritize cloud sync if possible--------------------------------------------------PART 6: UX REQUIREMENTS--------------------------------------------------Keep it simple.This should not feel bloated.Must have:- one-page dashboard layout- fast input- simple KPI cards- reverse-engineering section- gamification section- clean stats/history section- mobile-friendly navigation if neededNice additions:- save historical KPI snapshots by day, week, or month- weekly summary card- monthly summary card- simple progress chart- export data to CSV- local backup or cloud backup--------------------------------------------------PART 7: TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS--------------------------------------------------Build it as:- responsive HTML/CSS/JavaScript or React- minimal dependencies- clean code- organized file structureProvide:- complete code- file structure- setup instructions- explanation of how KPI calculations work- explanation of how reverse engineering works- explanation of XP, levels, and streak logic- explanation of cloud sync if implemented- explanation of how to run locally--------------------------------------------------IMPORTANT UX DETAILS--------------------------------------------------Please make the app:- simple- smooth- motivating- easy to use daily- visually relaxing- clear on both iPhone and desktopDo not overcomplicate it.Focus on:1. 4 KPI calculations2. reverse engineering3. streaks + XP + levels4. tropical Bali-themed design5. desktop + iPhone usability6. cloud sharing if possible

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content: If your offer is a one liner like "I take self-improvement coaches from $0 to $10K per month in 42 days with my 'Reverse Engineered $10K Method' or they don't pay" then you do tell it even to your neighboor because you lose nothing and create massive curiousity by putting it out there every time you open your mouth.Creating content is the slowest and most inefficient way to get calls but it's the best for the long-term, be prepared to do it consistently and daily if possible for 7 months to 3 years.Cold and warm outreach is the fastest and most powerful way because you get to target precisely your niche, but that, of course, assumes you have locked in PERFECTLY your niche, you know exactly how they look, where they hang out, what's their language and their desired outcome. You need to be able to describe their day to day from the top of your head if I were to ask you at a moment's notice.Running ads with a proven startegy and making less thatn $50K per month is basically insanity, I have seen it a thousand times and some of the best players in the market keep saying it, if you don't have $10K every month that you can literally BURN and forget about them testing different startegies for ads you are throwing money into the trash bin.The ICP is still too broad and doesn't give emotional triggers to the potential audience but it's a good place to start, it's definetely better than nothing.This is the no bullshit answer and I know it sounds harsh but I hope you can take it objectively and analyze these points.I hope it helps, brother.Delusion backed up just by words it's just delusion but delusion backed up by relentless action... that's called FAITH, brotner! Remember: Duty, Honor and Pride!!!

original_post.author: Lea Newkirk
original_post.title: Protecting the Focus.
original_post.body: I’ve been reading Deep Work by Cal Newport (recommended by @Scott Northwolf ), and it’s shifted how I look at the goals we’ve been reverse-engineering together. It made me realize that if we want to hit those big targets we talk about on our Friday calls, we can't afford to have our attention fractured. We’re all putting in so much work to stay in a high-frequency state, but I’ve noticed how easily "busy-ness" and multitasking can pull us right back down into the noise. To manifest the version of life we’re aiming for, I’m learning that we have to be just as intentional with our focus as we are with our vision.  I’m curious, what’s your take on this? Do you feel like multitasking is a necessary skill for where we're going, or is it just a distraction from the deep work we're actually here to do?

comment.content: I love to see this... keep up the good work, Lea. We are just getting started.

original_post.author: Lea Newkirk
original_post.title: ◀️Moving Backward
original_post.body: I’m still sitting with what @Scott Northwolf taught on the call about Reverse Engineering our goals, and honestly, it’s already changing my life. Instead of looking at where I am now and trying to climb the mountain toward a goal, I’ve been practicing sitting in the frequency of already having achieved it, and then looking back at the steps I took to get there. It shifts everything from "I hope I can do this" to "it is already a done." It makes the path feel so much more inevitable and way less overwhelming.  I’m curious, what’s one goal you’re currently reverse-engineering? If you look back from the "finished" version of your goal, what was the very first step you took to get there?

original_post.author: Scott Northwolf
original_post.title: Don't try to escape 😵‍💫
original_post.body: If you are trying to escape the present moment you are failing in real time.  The masculine error lies on believing that things will be fundamentally different some day.  "Someday my women will stop complaining."  "Someday I'll make enough money and I'll finally be free."  "Someday I'll land a client and everything will change forever."  Guess what?  When you land a client...  You still got to keep doing outreach.  You still got to keep building upwards.  I know because it happened to me.  Don't make the mistake of trying to escape your current situation.  If you stay on this path for long enough you WILL become rich and you WILL miss the days in which you were struggling and striving to hit these goals.  Enjoy the process now.  I know it sounds like a cliche but if you do that and let go of all atatchment to the outcome the inputs will become effortless and everything will flow without frustration.  Delusion backed up just by wo

I love learning from the way of the samurai. original_post.body: Hey, I'm Kamil. I've been on self-improvement for years and became a self-improvement coach this January. I got on self-improvement to leave my depression-inducing uni routine. original_post.author: Scott Northwolf
original_post.title: How does your day looks like?
original_post.body: I’m curious to know… how does your day looks like?  Mine looks like this: 06:00 Wake up. 06:00 - 07:00 Meditation and gratitude journaling. 07:00 - 08:00 Reading (Principles by Ray Dalio). 08:00 - 09:00 Working out. 09:00 - 09:30 Cold shower. 10:00 - 14:00 Coaching calls. 14:00 - 15:00 Lunch (typically with a friend) 15:00 - 17:00 Deep work (or sales’ calls). 17:00 - 19:00 Coaching calls. 19:00 - 20:00 Dinner or Sales’ calls. 20:00 - 21:00 Winding down routine. 21:00 - 21:21 Sleep.  Not judging but if you have a timetable you’re a legend and got my respect. 🫡  PS: Yes, the picture is of little houses for roosters. None related at all but I saw them and I thought it was funny as fuck! So there you go… here in Bali even roosters are living their best lives with their own private villas. What are you waiting for to join us??? Jack is already coming… @Jack Shiller

comment.author: Rob Nölken
comment.content: Depends a bit, but usually I wake up with the birds, sometimes meditate but for sure gratitude. Before I go to work I be on Skool and do my emails. Then go to work. Never know what time I am done. In the evening taking a walk, do some more work (website, email and accounting) then hit the bed early . Before I go to sleep I always meditate. Even if there was a party, I always do meditate.

replies.author: Scott Northwolf
replies.content: @Rob Nölken I love the flow… sounds quite natural and effortless.There are two main modes of approaching your routine (neither is better, they depend on what’s your goal and what are you optimizing for):1-Intentional routine (a routine you design yourself with your goals in mind).Pros: you can reverse engineer your goal into actionable steps that you can take every day and it’s impossible to not hit it with enough time and understanding how to reiterate your process and take in feedback).Cons: It can suffocate inspiration if not properly managed or balanced with seasons of rest.2-Unintentional routine (it’s basically the routine that forms when we are adapting [effectively and productively or ineffectively and counterproductively] to our environment and internal state).Pros: Great for creativity and inspiration.Cons: It can turn into instant gratification, chaos and too much of a lack of structure that doesn’t allow for consistency or the creation and maintenance of anything (so goals are quite hard to hit in this way.And of course you can even have a combination of the two…

With client sessions i also get a fair amount of my own practices in while instructing them digitally. Rapping/singing/dancing(a lot of my own tension relief and recovery comes from those three). All this really happens in a flow throughout the day in between computer work as is needed to stay balanced and focused. Sleep time about 21-23.

original_post.author: Andrew Brooks
original_post.title: Food quality in restaurants: probably not for you
original_post.body: How often do you go out, eat somewhere, and think to yourself "well, that wasn't great." And how did you feel afterwards?  There are reasons for the generally low quality of food in most restaurants. Mostly they are limited by their food budget. And the food budget limits the quality they are able to purchase because we also generally want cheap food. What that means is that most of the produce will be conventional produce, not organic. The meats (a large part of the budget) will be of lower quality and the rest will be medium quality at best.  The things we WANT: organic, fresh, healthy, and delicious, simply are not available within the cheap food framework. Unfortunately to get the good stuff you have to pay more. That's just the way it is for now...31Last comment Feb 24

In 2019 I got engaged to my partner of 7 years (aged 27)  3. We got a dog (2019) 4. COVID happened (2020)  5. We bought some land (2020) 6. October 2020 I was hit by a car   7. We built a house, got married, moved cities and changed jobs (2021)  8. 2021 cracks started forming in the relationship  9. 2022 Christmas I broke down about my mental heath challenges, my physical rehab journey and...  10. 2023 (January) she told me she was leaving...  11. I nearly took my life... pills left over from my car accident in 2020... but my dog walked in the room and I said fuck... I can't do that to you.  12. 2023 the house was sol

comment.author: Scott Northwolf

original_post.author: Sajjad Bablu
original_post.title: From Content → Conversations → Real Change (What I’m Testing Right Now)
original_post.body: Over the last weeks I’ve been studying something interesting:  Most people online are creating content for visibility Very few are creating systems for transformation  Likes don’t change a person. Motivation videos don’t change behavior. Even good advice rarely sticks.  So I started experimenting with a different approach.  Instead of posting random self-improvement content, I’m building a structured flow:  1. A problem people recognize immediately 2. A guided reflection 3. A daily actionable step 4. Consistency over motivation  The goal isn’t engagement. The goal is clarity + behavior change.  Right now I’m testing how people respond when the focus shifts from inspiration → guidance  Not selling anything here — just observing behavior patterns and learning what actually helps people follow through in real life.  Curious: Do you think people actually want transformat

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content: This is very interesting, brother. Hope it goes well and please do share what you find out with this approach.Personally I have discovered that people do not value what they get for free for the most part.True transformation I have only seen it taking place through high ticket programs because then they are putting skin on the game, they are committed to make a change and there's real world consecuences to them not doing so (especially if you structure your offer with a powerful full money back conditional guarantee or even a risk reversal one which are usually the best but only when you get to a certain level on the game).This is a clear breakdown of it with some estimate projections that reflect this point:📊 Success (Completion) Rates by Program TypeProgram TypeTypical Completion Rate (Success Proxy)Free programs~5–15 % complete Low-ticket programs (e.g., paid but no heavy support)~15–40 % complete High-ticket programs (e.g., with coaching/community support)~60–80 %+ completeI actually took the time to dig into this data because I have been seeing this patter for a while and I was wondering if there were any studies about it and there are!It's insane but it's true!Real transformation happens when people are in enough pain so their current situation and staying in it is actually more painful than the effort and sacrifice of taking action consistently and changing radically their lives.I hope understanding this helps you to focus your efforts on the right direction, brother, because I did struggle with this for quite a while, running a lot of free case studies that would end up with VERY high churn and barely any results out of a lack of work and consistency from my mentees but reality is how it is, and I could have sat down and complained about it but instead I decided to accept reality as it is and actually look for the most effective approach to make a meaningful and profound impact on people's lives.If you want to make such impact you either need insane amounts of traffic so this low completion rates get to represent a lot more people than on low traffic enviroments, or you have to switch to a model in which the low traffic is actually very successful because the system is built in such a way that is conductive to VERY high standards and results driven by the level of committment of people (which is what I do and what I personally focus on).Delusion backed up by words it's just delusion but delusion backed up by relentless action... that's called FAITH, brother. Remember: Duty, Honor and Pride!

replies.author: Sajjad Bablu
replies.content: @Scott Northwolf Good point, I agree commitment level changes behavior.Right now I’m intentionally starting with value and trust first to understand people deeply and see real patterns. Once the right structure is clear, monetization and commitment layers can be added on top of it.I’m testing the foundation before scaling the pressure.Will definitely share the results here.

original_post.author: Scott Northwolf
original_post.title: 🥇Talking about providing value...
original_post.body: Focus on providing as much value as possible and the best way to do that is by following this methodology:  1-Pick a specific niche. 2-Conduct niche research calls to figure out their most painful percieved problem and their desire outcome. 3-Develop the offer and methodology to deliver results (make sure to include their desired outcome, a time frame and a strong guarantee [conditional to them doing the work so you get their full commitment and better results] (you can read $100M Offers by Alex Hormozi for this). 4-Start doing sales' calls for this high ticket coaching/consulting offer and track your KPIs (Key Performance Metrics) [and also develop a sales' script, test it at least 30 times before you reiterate, you can read The Way of the Wolf by Jordan Belford for script building]. 5-Record your calls and analyze them to improve. 6-Reverse engineer the KPIs into daily inputs required to hit the money goal (of course,

original_post.title: 🧠 Limiting beliefs regarding money 💸
original_post.body: Have you stopped to ask yourself if you're holding any limiting beliefs regarding money that might be hindering your ability to make it?  I'll ask you some questions to see if that's the case so you can reflect on it:  1. Are you afraid of charging high ticket for your services as a coach?  2. Do you struggle to say clearly what you offer and how much you charge when asked?  3. Are you afraid of paying for mentorship yourself when you see a truly valuable offer out there?  36Last comment Feb 1

I am still looking for some

comment.author: Aeon Bancuyo
comment.content: I have all of this problems

replies.author: Scott Northwolf
replies.content: @Aeon Bancuyo alright! So, let's break it down:You are afraid of charging high ticket.Why is that?Is it because you feel like your services are no valuable enough?Then tell me...How much do you feel comfortable with charging (no number is wrong, even if it's 0 at the moment)?Now...whatever that number is...Think...how can you make that number 10 times higher?What kind of MASSIVE value could you provide in order to make it worth that amount of money for people?That's a first step towards reframing this limiting belief.It's a basic framework but it will give you an idea of how much you can improve in order to be able to charge the kind of prices that would not just change your life but your clients' lives as well, because the higher ticket they pay the higher the percieved value they'll see in your offer, the higher their commitment and the higher their likelihood of achieving their desired outcome.The second question is a two part thing.First comes clarity in what you offer and then comes the confidence to state the value behind it.Without that clarity is very hard to express confidently how much are you charging for it, because it's hard to see the value when things are chaotic and confusing and confidence is nothing but a proxy for competence.So we start by actually having a VERY well defined outcome (that aligns with the percieved pain/desired of your prospects) enticing enough for the people in your niche (of course, there are certain foundations to this like picking properly the niche, conducting niche research interviews to actually understand in depth the percieved pain your potential clients have and their desired outcome, but I'll be talking in more depth about the roadmap to $10K per month that we follow here on self-improvement Nation soon to make all of this more clear, I've been asked already a lot for this and it's going to be very useful to our members who haven't spoke to me frequently).With regards to the third one.The fact that you are afraid of paying for mentorship is not really about mentors out there (even though obviusly like in all market there are good ones and shittier ones) but it speaks about how you view yourself and ties back to the fear of having people paying you for your services because you don't believe yet your services are that valuable or perhaps that you can deliever effectively, and if you don't believe (yet) in your own coaching... how can you believe in someone else's?This is just the beginning but I hope it does offers you some clarity and makes you reflect in depth about this subject.I'd recommend deep journaling about it and if you have any questions feel free to reach out, Aeon.Like I said, there's a lot more but take it step by step. It's all about consistency.Delusion backed up just by words it's just delusion but delusion backed up by relentless action... that's called faith, brother! Remember: Duty, Honor and Pride!!!

replies.author: Aeon Bancuyo

original_post.author: Scott Northwolf
original_post.title: Are you restless❓
original_post.body: Radical change doesn't happen in a vacuum.  Radical change only happens when the pain of staying the same outweights the pain of change.  There's a certain kind of restlessness that is required for such change to take place. Sleepless nights, doubts, fears... frustration? Meeting with reality and having to accept the world is simply NOT how you thought it was. So what will you do now? Take it as insight and modify your behavoir to actually get what you want or stay down, in regret, failure...  People think Self-Improvement is a walk of roses, all about positive mentality and some soft meditation but this is a FUCKING WAR TO THE DEATH with your old self.  There's no pleasure without pain and too much pleasure in the end will bring more pain than you could ever had imagine.  What do you really want out of this life?  Have you ever stopped to ask that to yourself?  And if so...  Have you ever wondered why?  I'll be reading VE

original_post.author: Paul Hedron
original_post.title: Basic Leadership Primer
original_post.body: The basic components of a team are as follows:  A group of individuals, a vision, and a facilitator to direct effort and attention.  A leader must be an expert in the nature of the vision, the individuals on the team, and the conditions in which the vision and team interact.  Without a vision or goal, the collection of individuals cannot coalesce. Without individuals to coalesce, a vision or goal is without potential and impetus. Without understanding of the conditions, a team will experience unnecessary inertia, spending effort to achieve the vision.  A vision is merely a blueprint, something to build. The individuals on the team are the builders. The leader is the architect and facilitator.  The base component of a team and shared vision is the individual.  By understanding the individual, a leader has better command of complexity.  An individual has a capable skillset, and limitations. Understanding both the capability/capacity,

comment.author: Paul Hedron
comment.content: @Scott Northwolf @Tony Palmer @Nick Steep @Jack Shiller @Flow Z @Andrea PirasLet me know if this is useful.

replies.author: Paul Hedron
replies.content: @Scott Northwolf Hey man, yeah, so let's get into this:What I, personally, primarily focus on is producing a "meta-leader"; a leader that produces other leaders.We can look at the basic outline as something like this:Follower - Does what is toldContributor - Contributes autonomouslyCoordinator/Manager - Organizes, controls, and orders workflowLeader - Focus on relationships between individuals and environments/contextsTransformational leader - Focus on growth, development, and capability cultivationSystems leader - Focus on structure and process for scalingMeta-Leader - Leadership development across scales and contextsAnything beyond Meta-Leader is architectural in nature; designing systems and environments where leadership characteristics arise organically, designing systems that produce those systems, designing organized cultural systems that allow for the previously mentioned systems.Beyond that would be existential and civilizational in scale: long-term and broad scopes resulting in overall evolution, and existential emergence. At this scale, think of something like designing an autonomous system where people and individuals could utilize AI to innovate and infinitely scale emergence and emergent properties.I think the natural progression of anyone that gets into self improvement and consulting will inevitably reach some level of meta-leadership, it just doesn't necessarily get noticed.The natural start is transformation leadership; helping an individual essentially express themselves. Not in the way "express yourself" is typically meant; more in an ecological sense. Become the role and ecological niche someone is meant to fill. I started this with alternative therapy, rehabbing "at risk youth" and delinquent adults (criminals, or people at risk of becoming criminal). The individuals I worked with generally had no positive way to integrate into society, so the general gameplan was to identify what they are good at, what they naturally want to do, and find ways to do that, legally, and positively for broader society. A violent guy, for example, is bad in the context of a mugger. In the context of a bouncer or a cop, however, that becomes a useful and sanctioned positive. Same expression of violence, different context.From there, the only natural progressions are focusing on systems that allow for these types of individuals to positively work together, or developing the individuals themselves to be autonomous positive role models.Unfortunately, what I found, and what I was frustrated with, is that the systems that were helping the individuals I liked to work with, also supported individuals that effectively didn't have "real" problems. Weird nerds, derpy kids, people trying to "be cool". Additionally, supporting an individual into being a positive role model requires some level of external support for both the meta-leader, and the individuals they are trying to support. The inevitable outcome of this was cyclical collapse of individuals and systems not properly designed to achieve this.A former violent criminal needs guys that want to live positive lives, and the actual systems, resources, and support to do this.It simply doesn't, or didn't exist at the time.However, the basic skillset is scalable and applicable to other contexts and domains.The tangible outcome; this is basically the same process of self-improvement, extended to its maximum ends. Help an individual achieve mastery in any and every context they exist within, or could exist within.So, in some sense, yes, decentralized cells, or decentralized autonomous organizations(s).In that way, it's not isolated to one tribe, so to speak, but either any tribe, new tribes, or the ability to start tribes.It depends on the context and intent.Specifically, for meta-leadership, that'd mostly be about starting new tribes entirely, or teaching the skills to start a new tribe. From there, you'd have a hierarchy of sub-system leadership that doesn't extend past the boundaries of the tribe.Personally, this is what I do, in some sense or another. I don't align to any one specific group for extended periods, merely because I've identified that there simply needs to be more positive influences and places for people to go.I see this as natural expression at play; if an individual wants to be a leader, then they don't want to be a follower of someone else. They want to express traits of developing their own tribe.At that level, this does start to become more about governing systems and civilizational architecture. So the extension leaves leadership, entirely, and becomes about building something that leaders of various types can actually use and want to use.In some sense, that's also sort of what you're doing, now; uniting and aligning leaders together in a loose coalition or syndicate. Not necessarily all working on the same projects, but multiple projects aligning, together.That's probably more accurate to what I actually do, or attempt to do; help people become someone like you; capable of leading, designing, uniting, and operating, semi-autonomously, but for unified purpose. So, designing systems that produce individuals capable of producing syndicates. That scale is difficult, though, and this type of person is exceptionally rare, and so it naturally defaults down into the meta-leadership level. A syndicate can't form, unless the initial leader is already a meta-leader. That's sort of rare and difficult, but it's certainly doable.So, I just start there.It's also natural default expression for me; I prefer to be around others capable of being entirely independent across scale and context. That would normally be the initial seed of a syndicate, but I simply don't have the interest in sort of stopping, prematurely. The next logical progression would be some level of meta-syndication, creating multiple large aligned organizational networks, and then connecting those networks. That seems to be the scale I want to work towards, but that doesn't necessarily exist, so I can't comment too deeply. I'd have to see it, to know if that's the correct scale I express towards.But yeah, absolutely, different scales and purposes for different people. The same fundamental dynamics exist at every scale, though, so realistically, even imparting any level of leadership capability, even into someone that wants to remain something like a follower or manager, either the natural trait expression exists, or it doesn't. You'd just end up with a super potent, capable follower, with no expression of broader scale leadership interests; they are comfortable being a manager, which is going to operate more effectively than someone who is forced into management, but wants to express natural leader traits. Matching container and component size; a big fish in a small pond, something like that. A lot of solid guys actually prefer to be at the level of follower, merely because there simply aren't guys worth leading, or there aren't things to lead towards. So, it does become a very interesting dynamic to see play out over scales and contexts. That does lead into my fascination with broader architecture; if the conditions existed for broadscale trait expression, what would happen?I simply have no idea, so I simply default down into the meta-leadership layer, because that's about as good as it gets.

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content: When you talk about turning individuals into leaders, do you mean creating something like individual autonomous cells, like a decentralized organization?The thing is, I believe this can be either interpreted as giving enough understanding and power to each individual so they develop themselves and become truly useful members for the tribe OR as pseudo-anarchic kind of organization, a decentralized thing, kind of like the internet phenomenon of Anonymous.With the first interpretation, building upwards members of the tribe and getting them to a place of taking absolute personal responsibility for their lives, being capable of autonomously and sustainably grow and have their own systems in place for their development and for yielding consistently results for themselves and for moving forwards the cause of the tribe… that’s powerful and aligns with my vision and naturally will always align with the vision of any self-improvement mentor.But decentralized structures and multiple cells of command only work for very specific kinds of purposes and environments.I don’t believe we have the need to over-think all this at the moment.Things like culture, organization and logistics and politics tend to be better put into practice by empirical testing and exploration, especially at the beginning stages.

original_post.author: Aeon Bancuyo
original_post.title: What do you do to keep being social?
original_post.body: I’m curious do you have friends or do you interact often with people not on self improvement?  I thought about the picture of the about page that @Scott Northwolf has, and I would love to have that kind of friend group as well.  Honestly, I’ve been in pain my whole life for not having a “stable” friend group, is this normal?  My friends circle we’re only 3 at a time, and it would die off overtime.  Currently, having not deep relationships with my school friends.  There’s so many people who I can be friends with in school, it’s too easy.  But I have a limiting belief that, theyre not worth it. Honestly, I get the “ick” when I’m trying to fit in to the gossip conversations, or the shallow “issue” of the day.  What do you do to battle this, I have a two friends that I have a deep relationship with but not at school.  What do you do to keep being social, even with people outside of self imorovemnt?35Last comment Jan 29

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content: I'm not really social with people who are not into self-improvement to be honest. The beauty about Bali is that EVERYONE (or at least most people) are into self-improvement so that makes it perfect but I know this is a very rare place, that's also the reason I moved here.Back in the day, when I was still back home I had a big group of friends but when I got into self-improvement I just stopped having things in common with them, our interests just weren't the same and even though I tried to get them on self-improvement I failed. You cannot force people to improve their lives if they don't want to.So basically I endured the time in solitude and used it to not be afraid of being alone and carve my own path, I also used it as motivation to make a shit ton of money and start traveling the world in order to find my tribe which I successfully did here in Bali.On self-improvement you'll go through this phase of loneliness in which you are not high status enough yet to be friends with the people you want to be friends in real life because they are still way above you but if you use that as motivation to get to their level you will get there, it's just a matter of time and commitment.I know it's hard but it's worth it and there's no going back once you take the red pill anyways.The only option is to keep moving forward, brother. Use this community to remind yourself of the people you'll have around you when you make it to the other side and don't lose faith.A lot of us are living here already and we have fulfilled the dream we were shooting for... now we are building together towards a higher aim and I can tell you there's nothing better than making your dreams come true with effort, sacrifice and dedication.

replies.author: Aeon Bancuyo
replies.content: @Scott Northwolf thanks for this man!I'll keep this as a reminder, but do you think there's a way for me to be not a social nerd and just make myself isolated, whilst on self improvement?

replies.author: Scott Northwolf
replies.content: @Aeon Bancuyo the funny thing about social skills is that you can actually develop them more by developing yourself and creating a rock solid confidence born out of REAL competence than by simply "doing reps" and talking to normies non-stop. It's the highest leverage thing you can do. By developing this sense of pride on yourself and having high standards for the people around you, counter-intuitively you become magnetic to people. I know the instant gratification pull of being social is strong but I can promise you that locking in and developing yourself as a man will only bring net benefits to your life and will not turn you into a "social weirdo" or a "self-improvement nerd" umless you hold such limiting belief and turn it into a self fulfilling profecy.

comment.author: Andrew Brooks
comment.content: I'm not really "social" either. I have about 3 good friends in my locale, and I have others that I connect with virtually every week. But I do feel the need to be social often.So, I invite friends over for dinner, plant to meet up for a coffee or a movie, or just meet up to hang out somewhere for an hour. It doesn't have to be a big thing.But I do plan fun things intentionally. I keep track of the artsy/cultural things happening in my area and will often just show up to some of those and see if I can chat with a new person there. And I will sometimes invite people I meet to connect with me at the event. Sometimes they show, sometimes not. But I'm ok doing things by myself too.So don't feel weird. You aren't. Especially as we get older, the number of friends typically dwindles, but the QUALITY of the friendships goes up. And that's ok.

replies.author: Scott Northwolf
replies.content: @Andrew Brooks very grounded approach.

I just saw a wonderful video by one of our members ( @Laurits Valentin Offersen ) and was delighted to see this poweful mindset at play. original_post.author: Laurits Valentin Offersen
original_post.title: Be full of yourself, be delusional.
original_post.body: I’m 18 years old and I’m fully aware that, on paper, I’m not that impressive yet.  I don’t have crazy money.I’m not a finished product.I’m still early.  But here’s the part most people won’t admit:  I’m delusional about where I’m going.  Not in an ego way, in a commitment way.I know I’m getting there because I’ve already decided I won’t stop.  That belief is what gets me up at 5 am  That belief is what keeps me working when no one’s watching.  That belief is what makes quitting impossible.  People call it arrogance.  I call it certainty.  If I didn’t believe that strongly in my future self, I’d move like everyone else, procrastinating, Hesitating and Half-committed.  But I don’t need proof yet.  I just need to stay consistent.  Being “delusional” is only a problem when you don’t work.When you do the work, it becomes inevitable.  if you do average ammounts of work you become average  if you do exceptional

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content: Delusion backed up just by words it’s just delusion, but delusion backed up by relentless action… that’s called faith, brother! Remember: Duty, Honor and Pride!!!

original_post.author: Scott Northwolf
original_post.title: The three components of the Meta-skill 🎯
original_post.body: 1. Deliberate Practice. 2. Reflection upon your practice. 3. Theoretical knowledge applied to your practice.  Deliberate practice:  To become a grand master at chess the first thing you do is start playing. Once you have been playing for a while you will reach a certain level of skill. Let’s call it level 1. You have completed level 1 of that skill (obviously this is a simplistic way of seeing it, it’s hard to pinpoint theoretically at which point you have actually achieved the highest level of skill you could obtain from practice alone, but combined with the other components of the meta-skill you can advance way faster). Then you’ll realize that to keep getting better you’ll need something else. You’ll hit a ceiling. Then comes the next level…  Reflection upon your practice:  If you start analyzing every single move you make, write it down, pay attention to every move your opponent makes, tr

original_post.body: 1-Without a timetable you are like a captain on a ship trying to get to his destination with no map or compass. If you allow yourself to be guided by the winds (AKA your current situation, mood, feelings and lower based desires) you are going to end up making very poor choices.  Instant gratification will always be your place of choice and productivity, achievements and actual fulfillment will evade you forever. Also, you need to realize that relaying on willpower instead of leveraging a system to build habits it’s just straight up stupid, especially if you know how willpower and decision fatigue works. Let’s explain this on a very comprehensible and non scientific manner, let’s call it bro-science, like Hamza, the big brother of self improvement himself calls it:  When you wake up you’ve got a certain amount of “brain points”, each decision you make, even a small one like choosing wha

original_post.title: How to Set Goals Like a Millionaire (and actually achieve them)
original_post.body: The first thing you need to realize is that if you don't have a goal that is your north star you are like a ship in the middle of the ocean, what surrounds you is the uncertain, chaos, and until you have a goal, you will not know what's the actual direction you want to go, what's your port of destination, with a destination you can build order out of chaos. Thinking from first principles is one of the most powerful things you can do, setting your goal through a process of deep journaling and profound inquiry is the thing that will bring the most clarity in your life. So... how do we go about setting ourselves a proper goal? First we ask to ourselves "What do I want out of life?" Chances are, if you are on this community you want to make money online. But what really means to make money online? Being an educational content creator? What it means is freedom. We want freedom to be able to ch

original_post.title: Does anyone know how to become ambitious?

comment.author: Zakh Chowdhury
comment.content: I've consumed 100s of books, podcasts, articles, and courses on neuroscience, metaphyiscs, psychology, and manifestation and here is my viewpoint on developing ambition:Most people think they lack ambition, but what they actually lack is clarity, and those are two very different problems. Ambition isn’t something you force, it’s something that naturally shows up when you’re moving toward the right thing. The issue is that most of us are chasing goals that were never really ours in the first place. They’re inherited from social media, family expectations, or whatever environment we’ve been in, so even if they sound good on paper, they feel heavy when you actually try to pursue them. That’s why you can feel stuck, unmotivated, or constantly second guessing yourself. It’s not because you’re lazy, it’s because there’s a misalignment between what you’re chasing and what actually resonates with you.The first thing you have to do is remove pressure. When you tell yourself you need to figure everything out or you have to be successful, you create this internal tension that actually blocks clarity. You start overthinking, comparing, and forcing decisions instead of seeing things clearly. When you loosen that grip and shift into a mindset of choosing rather than needing, things start to open up. You think better, you notice more, and you’re not operating from fear. From there, you need to separate what you genuinely want from what you think you should want. A simple way to do that is to ask yourself if no one could see your life, no recognition, no validation, would you still choose this path? If the answer is no, then it’s probably not your goal. Another good check is how it feels when you think about it. The right direction usually feels energizing, even if it’s challenging. The wrong one feels draining, even if it’s achievable.Once you start filtering things this way, you can begin building a clear vision, not just of outcomes, but of the type of person you want to be and the kind of life you want to live day to day. Most people get stuck because they only think in terms of results, like money or status, but clarity actually comes from seeing yourself in motion, your habits, your environment, your work, your lifestyle. When that picture becomes clear, your brain starts recognizing opportunities that align with it, and decisions become easier because you’re not guessing anymore. At that point, instead of relying purely on discipline, you start following energy. Pay attention to what you naturally gravitate toward, what you think about without forcing it, what feels meaningful even when it’s hard. That’s usually where your real path is. It might not look logical at first, but it’s where consistency becomes effortless because you’re not fighting yourself.Ambition, then, isn’t about grinding harder or pushing yourself endlessly. It’s about locking into a direction that pulls you forward. When you’re aligned, you don’t need constant motivation, you don’t question everything, and you don’t feel like you’re forcing your life to happen. Things start to click because your actions, your thoughts, and your direction are all working together instead of against each other. So if you feel stuck right now, don’t try to become more ambitious. Focus on getting clear. Strip away the noise, remove the pressure, and start paying attention to what actually feels true for you. Because the right goals don’t need to be chased, they create their own momentum, and once you find those, ambition stops being something you struggle with and becomes something you naturally embody.Lmaoooo sorry for the long wordy post but hopefully this helps anyone that sees it :)

comment.content: What worked for me was increasing my intrinsic motivation. It might sound super weird but this is usually what happens when we try to reach our goals:Extrinsic motivation sparks action → Action sparks flow → Flow boosts intrinsic motivation.Extrinsic motivation is things outside of us like money fame etc. When we take action, that puts us in flow state and that boosts our intrinsic motivation which is our internal reasons for doing something.What really helped me increase my intrinsic motivation is having clear goals and a clear why like the above comments said as well :)

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content: 3 things:1-Goal setting, drive vacuums identification and reverse engineering of your goal into actionable steps.2-After identifying your goal get inspiration and information about it (e.g. if you want to become a millionaire read the biographies of Andrew Carnegie and Rockefeller, if you want to become a military genious read Alexander's Campagins by Arian and Napoleon: A life).3-Identify your current beliefs with regards to the components of reality that you are struggling with and flip those beliefs in their head, build then a Self-Transcendence Document or Alchemy of Self Workbook.If you want me to explain in more depth those things just shoot me a DM. I know the third one is the "weirdest" one but it requires a LOT of explanation so it makes sense...The logical chain is like this:Experiences form beliefs.Beliefs form your identity.Your identity informs your paradigm (the lenses through which you see the world and interpret it).Your paradigm will inform your percieved options.Your percieved options will inform your actions (you can only make a decision out of the percieved options you believe are available to you).Your actions will solidify (only temporarily) into your behaviour (habits, routines [intentional or unintentional]).Your behaviour will determine your results.

replies.author: Michelle Martiya
replies.content: @Brandon Maloney Many trainers like to play what we call the "clicker game" with their human learners to help them understand how animals learn.So pick a reinforcer (Skittles, M&M's, quarters, etc.). Give your human learner a cup. Have them move randomly around the room. Whenever they get any approximation towards the goal behavior (moving toward the chair for "sit"), put a reinforcer in their cup. Keep reinforcing each small approximation until they figure out that you want them to sit. This is positive reinforcement. Alternately (don't do this unless you've done #1 first, or your human learner will quit), you can pick a sound that's super annoying, like fingernails on a chalkboard. Play the sound continuously until your human learner gets an approximation towards the goal behavior. After a second or two, start playing the sound again until they get another approximation. Continue until they figure out that you want them to sit. This is negative reinforcement.You can use the same sound, but this time, whenever your human learner gets the wrong answer (moves away from the chair), play the sound briefly. Keep doing this until they figure out that you want them to sit in the chair. Caution: your friend may hate you after this one. This is positive punishment. Lastly, fill your human learner's cup with one of those reinforcers from the first lesson. Every time they get the wrong answer, take away a few reinforcers. Keep doing this until they figure out that you want them to sit. This is negative punishment. Good luck, and I'm gonna need video of all of this! 😅Here's an example of the first one: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=c1eda0XmoLkNobody ever demos the others because they make human learners feel bad, which is ironic, since those are the ones people use most with their animals.2:05

original_post.author: Michael Pluszek
original_post.title: Soul vs Identity
original_post.body: I posted a question about identity earlier in Synthesizer community, and it sparked some great conversations and perspectives.  Now another question came up that I’m curious about.  What is identity… and what is soul?  What’s the difference?  And how does a person actually know what is what?  Would love to hear your reflections and perspectives.

comment.author: Michael Pluszek
comment.content: I truly love these conversations, as reading your perspectives, helps me unlock new things. Thank you.

replies.author: Kenneth Khamphiphone
replies.content: @Lexi Cruz the idea that identity evolves but the soul stays constant is a strong frame. Almost like the soul is the stability that makes changing your identity safe.

replies.author: Lexi Cruz
replies.content: @Kenneth Khamphiphone I like that frame! If the soul provides the stability, it would explain why identity can evolve so much throughout life. It makes identity flexible because something deeper is holding everything together. Do you think people are aware of that stability, or only notice it after big identity shifts?

replies.author: Michael Pluszek
replies.content: @Lexi Cruz great question.I am very curious from your experience what is the soul then and where does it come from?

replies.author: Kenneth Khamphiphone
replies.content: @Lexi Cruz That's the part that makes identity less fragile once you see it. If the soul holds steady then losing an identity isn't losing yourself. More like a costume change than an ending.

replies.author: Lexi Cruz
replies.content: @Michael Pluszek The way I’ve been thinking about it is that identity is the character we build through roles and experiences, while the soul is the awareness behind that character. I’m still exploring where it comes from though. What’s your view? Do you see the soul as something spiritual, or more like awareness?

replies.author: Lexi Cruz
replies.content: @Kenneth Khamphiphone I like that way of describing it! Seeing identity as the character and the soul as the witness makes identity feel much less rigid. The character can evolve without feeling like you are losing yourself. It makes me wonder if a lot of identity struggles come from people thinking the character is the whole self, when it might just be one layer.

replies.author: Michael Pluszek
replies.content: @Lexi Cruz great way to explain the identity part.The soul seems to be more difficult to explain.The best way I can share how I see it/experience it as I sit here right now would be like comparing it to consciousnes.It's that deep part behind it all.

comment.content: (edited)Here’s a thought I’ve been playing with..Identity might be the roles and stories we attach to ourselves.But the soul might be the part of us that still exists even when those roles change.Identity evolves, but something deeper stays constant underneath it.Which makes me wonder, do you think the soul is something we discover, or something we slowly remember?And how do you view the soul and identity?

I've tried to apply the books & seminars and certain life lessons have expanded into greater awareness. Although I'm now working on not learning everything the hard way!What's been your greatest 'ah ha' moment? Mine has been the realisation I have two relationships: one with the material world - and one with myself/inner world. I'm not deeply religious, but developing a connection to the 'spiritual self' has taken the pressure off 'me' so to speak.

comment.author: Pip Simpson
comment.content: Hi Michael 👋🏼 I’m new here but felt compelled to jump in, love this question.My sense is that identity is the self we’ve built through habits, memories, roles, and unconscious conditioning. Because it’s often not consciously chosen, at least initially, it can be seen as elastic, trainable, and changeable. Usually it takes some kind of life experience to make us question it in the first place.To me, the soul is the deeper part of us that exists underneath all of that. Something transcendent, which may be connected, as Emerson suggests, to a greater or collective mind. Something that feels less like it is constructed and more like a truth to be uncovered or discovered.Our Identity can be inherited or protective. The concept of a soul feels more expansive, more alive, and less performative.And because the mind can imitate a lot, I think stillness matters. Breathwork, meditation, and energy work can all help us go within and sense what is patterned, and what feels more deeply true. I think developing understanding and awareness of both is key rather than forcing some sort of change.Would love to know your experience. What have you been contemplating recently?Pip

replies.author: Michael Pluszek

comment.author: Nick Banik
comment.content: For me, I think of Identity near the top of the NLP logical levels pyramid, but above it is Purpose/Spirituality. Basically I think of our soul as the higher self connected to God, which brings us our purpose that connects to our identity and then trickles down the other logical levels.We can change every logical level from Environment up to Identity, but when it comes to purpose and spirituality, we can work with those but they are deeply connected to the soul. A lot of the times, we are finding our purpose and spirituality versus changing it as something malleable. We are aligning with purpose whereas with our other levels like identity, we can change them with work.Identity is made up of our qualities, and qualities are made up of our values and what we believe to be true about ourselves. If we have misalignment in our values and qualities, our identity suffers and this is where stuff like imposter syndrome comes from. With work, we can adjust identity and align our qualities and values.Our soul is our connection to God and we cannot just change it, but we can align with our higher purpose, so I think any purpose work is about alignment and not actually about changing it. If you are pursuing something out of alignment with purpose, you will have to make changes to get in alignment with purpose and then you can get fulfillment.

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content: Alright! So let me break this down.For clarity and simplicities' sake I'll omit some details and break it down into a simplistic model developed by the ancient Hermetics.Reality is structured in different layers of organization.1-Source consciousness (the void, the one, what ancient Greeks in the Pythagorean tradition used to call the Monad).2-The Mental Plain (The Duat).3-The causal plain (where there's an observer, an object and a subject, you need three elements to have an experience, you need an object, a subject and an option, an experience itself).4-The Astral Plain (what's called the Quantum Field of Possibilities, where ideas, language, feelings and imagination exist as components of reality we can percieve).5-Physical reality (the manifestation and integration of all the above components).To understand identity and the soul we need to understand first this structure because it's foundational to the understanding that flows from this. If we don't take this as the basis the rest will not make sense and to explain why this is so it will take... well, a lot of time, so let's just suspend disbelief and accept this framework as a precodition for the following knowledge to unfold itself.If you want to get the full explanation just let me know and I'll write the second part of this...

comment.author: Christy Sanderson
comment.content: To me, identity is the story and structure we build around ourselves. It includes our roles, beliefs, values, skills, and the meaning we give to our experiences. Identity can evolve as we grow, learn, and consciously redesign how we show up in the world. It’s the lens through which we interpret life and decide what actions feel aligned.Soul, on the other hand, feels more like the deeper essence beneath all of that the quiet, consistent core of being. While identity can change with seasons, environments, and intentions, the soul feels more stable and foundational. It’s often sensed in moments of stillness, intuition, creativity, or when something simply feels profoundly “true.”The difference, in my view, is that identity is shaped and expressed, while soul is discovered and experienced. One is partly constructed; the other is more inherent.As for knowing what is what I think it comes through awareness and reflection. When something feels driven by expectation, comparison, or strategy, it may be identity at work. When it feels deeply grounding, meaningful, and quietly energizing, it may be closer to soul.

replies.author: Michael Pluszek
replies.content: @Christy Sanderson wauw Christy, your words here are amazing, I really appreciate you expressing them.And it makes so much sense.I would say it is how I see this too, but I could probably not articulate it as beautiful and simple as you have done here.Beautiful.Question, one thought that came up and I would love to get your view on.Lets say I feel totally excited about and opportunity and I am just compelled to go with it and/or let's say I feel totally attracted to a woman/just feeling love.Where would you say these emotions belonged to.Really curious about your perspective on this.

replies.author: Christy Sanderson
replies.content: @Michael Pluszek thank you so much for your kind words I truly appreciate your encouragement. That is a very thoughtful question. In my view, emotions like excitement, attraction, and feeling compelled toward an opportunity often arise first in the soul which includes our mind, will, and emotions. The soul responds to experiences, possibilities, desires, chemistry, memories, and hopes. These feelings are real and important because they give us information about what is happening within us. However, I believe it is also important to discern them through the lens of identity and deeper alignment. Identity helps us ask: Does this choice reflect who I am becoming? Does it align with my values, purpose, and the direction I sense my life is meant to go? Sometimes excitement is a signal to explore. Sometimes it is simply emotional momentum. Sometimes attraction is connection. Other times it may be projection or timing. This is why reflection, awareness, and grounding are so valuable they help us integrate what we feel with what we know to be true about ourselves. So I would say those emotions belong primarily to the soul, but they become wise and fruitful when they are processed through clarity of identity, intention, and conscious choice. Really appreciate you in this conversation.

replies.author: Michael Pluszek
replies.content: @Christy Sanderson thank you for your words and energy in this conversation Christy, I appreciate that.It's interesting for me (at least), that we actually never really know..Sometimes, I have followed the spark and synchronicities and it led me to so much joy, abundance etc.Other times, it led me to something I did not expect.But from those experience there was a lot of learning involved.So I chose to go with the believe, that the experience was there to serve me.But from personal experience, I more and more step into - I have no clue.. And follow the path that shows up and take it step by step.This is a new path - And one I have not stepped on before, so I am kind of expanding my life experience, by now stepping like this.So interesting it is.Thank you.

There is something so powerful about being willing to step into the unknown and trusting the process, even when we don’t fully understand where it will lead.I especially resonate with what you said about learning through experience and allowing each step to serve your growth. That takes courage and a deep level of self-awareness. Your reflections are always thoughtful and inspiring to read.I’m curious — what journey or life moments led you to begin focusing more intentionally on identity? replies.author: Michael Pluszek
replies.content: @Christy Sanderson thank you for being here - I enjoy your sharing, commenting, questions and your energy.I too am happy that you find this exciting, this journey of deeper expansion and growth.I still have an open invitation for you and I on a call - Just let me know when you are ready - Would love this.The answer to your question - Would be very long. I will do my best to take the short version here.I have always been like a seeker. A seeker of fun, joy, love, good stuff, knowledge, expansion, figuring what's possible, and probably in some form - Who am I.In 2012, I started a long personal development journey with seminars and coaching around the world. I followed my intuition and did what felt right and I learned/unlearned so much - And must important - I began to understand that I had an inner world and began to understand, that there was so much going on in that inner world, that I consciously had never been aware of. And a lot more is involved in this phase.So my start of that journey in 2012, came from me being in a 1.5 million dollar debt - And made a decision to not give up - But solve it (which I did).I did speakings, seminars, coaching about all the things that I learned, and found out, that I somehow had a gift of connecting with people and they would share their inner secrets and fears with me and I could ask them the right questions and it would "pop" something inside them and we could release.Maybe around 2014-2020 (not 100% sure) - After divorce with my daughters mother (who told me constantly how bad a person I was) - I found this amazing woman, who was everything I wished for (I thought) - And in the 6 years it lasted, I got more and more devoted to me being the best man I could and love her with all of my heart..Blindly...I didn't see all the signs.. The stop lights.. The warnings...I just kept going, and all. the tears she showed me, I thought was real and from suffering in early ages...Well.. Long story short.. After 6 years, I was drained, my head was screwed, no life force left...And I finally found out, that she had another man.. And one more kid than what I knew of.That cracked me.... Like a Coconut with the biggest shell..I cracked right open.. And my whole world, my whole being collapsed.. And I had no clue what was up or what was down for weeks.. I couldn't even walk or talk.. It was really crazy.From there I found the right people who helped me, with deep releases of traumas, of really deep energies frozen in the body, and from there began a journey going inward, what I call my dark phase and years, where I did nothing but being with me in silence and letting go of trauma/frozen/stuck energy one after the other.And I got really deep with all kind of experimental ways of going deeper.As I wrote this is the short version @Christy Sanderson 14 years of discovery - But - The 14 years are about all the years before that, starting from early on (and I could go even longer here and talk about experiences that went far longer than that).It's been a crazy ride but wouldn't be with out it.So I am curious about your story too here Christy.What got you interested in all of this on the inside.. What have you done etc.I am right here.

Going through debt, divorce, betrayal, and then choosing not to give up but to grow from it that says a lot about your character. I respect the fact that you kept seeking understanding and healing instead of becoming bitter. For me, my interest in the “inner world” also came through pain and life transitions. original_post.author: Mark Santos
original_post.title: What I learned after hitting 800 subscribers
original_post.body: Hey Synthesizers!  During the fall of 2024 I uploaded 7 videos for 12 weeks straight and ended up going from 200 -> 800 subscribers.  I did this while being a 27 year old married man, working a full time job.  After hitting 800 subscribers I noticed three things: - I was unsure about what to sell - I didn’t have a single client - I only booked one call  I was confused about what to do next.  I hopped on Skool and was reminded about a beautiful thing that exists.  “The Synthesizer Roadmap”  I put my trust in Andrew Kirby and hopped the following 5 months on 100 calls - 65 networking calls - 30 discovery calls - 5 sales calls  As a result I closed 2 online clients on Synthesized deals.  That’s because The Roadmap allowed me to get on calls and discover real life problems.  This is where I could leverage my proof in multiple areas and create customized solutions.  How far are you into The Roadmap are you right no

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content: So this is basically what I have done:1-Picked a niche (people I really wanted to help and I identified with because I was them in the past, they were self-improvement coaches because that's what I was myself).2-Started conducting niche research interviews to figure out what was their problem and their desired outcome (didn't try to assume it, actually asked).3-With all the info gathered I build my offer and methodology ensuring good product to market fit.4- I developed a systematic sales' system with cold and warm outreach here on skool and jumping on sales' calls with the sales' script I developed.5-Calculated my Key Performance Indicators and reverse engineered them into daily outreach volume to hit my desired income.6- Recorded my calls and reiterated it in order to improve.7- After 3 months of no results but pure consistency I landed in one week my three first high ticket clients and surpassed $10K... (it took a while to get the sales' script and the correct outreach volume right)I think that sums it up...

original_post.author: Lea Newkirk
original_post.title: 🌒Synthesis Requires Silence.
original_post.body: We often think of rest as a "reward" for being productive (most times I still do). I am starting to realize that’s just bad math. In the high-intensity environment of self-improvement, where we are constantly upgrading our internal software, if I don’t schedule the off time, my nervous system just schedules a burnout for me. Rest isn't the opposite of productivity, it’s the essential cooldown phase that allows the next sprint to happen. I mean, we wouldn't run a high-end processor at 100% capacity 24/7, it would melt. Our biology is no different. I’m curious, in an environment like this, how do you personally distinguish between "healthy friction" and a nervous system that’s starting to redline, and what are the specific warning signs you usually miss until it’s too late?

original_post.author: Hein van der Horst
original_post.title: I launched a Skool community today
original_post.body: I run an Instagram theme page in the business & finance niche. Nearly 13,000 followers in 3 months.  I noticed a pattern: a lot of my followers were creators and brands trying to grow on Instagram too.  So I built a free community around the strategy I used. No paid ads. Consistent content. A few collabs. That's it.  The community is called Instagram Creators Community. Free to join. Premium tier for the full breakdown. Still early. Still figuring out the monetization side. If you've launched a Skool community before. What moved the needle for you in the first 30 days?

comment.author: Ryan Grant
comment.content: Well done man !! Welcome to skool 😼You're in the right place 100%.Something that I did in the first month (I didn't have an audience) was reach out to family and friends to help make the initial founding members to help me kick start things. For a few months before that I was also doing networking on skool, providing value where I could and that helped me get some members in too when I told them I was making a community!One thing I didn't do early enough which I should of - is hop on more calls with members. Get as much feedback as you can, understand their current situation as much as you can.And the most important thing - don't be afraid to give away for free. We can never give too much away. Give give give give and the returns will be asymmetrical!How's things going so far?

replies.author: Hein van der Horst
replies.content: @Ryan Grant thanks man! No members so far. Just reading & trying to understand what to do.

replies.author: Ryan Grant
replies.content: @Hein van der Horst THAT'S THE SPIRIT!Good stuff. Through understanding you'll get all the members you need 😁👊What are you focusing on in terms of learning right now?

comment.content: The first step of something amazing man! I think the best thing you can do is just market it as much as possible on your socials. You've got a great advantage since you have followers :)

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content: 2 main things:1-Outreach on skool around communities filled with my niche (but for this you must have a cristal clear picture of your niche, there's a whole process I followed for this).2-Leveraging cognitive biases to attract the people from my niche knowing from my previous niche research interviews their desired outcome and main painpoints and designing my about page with those stimuli on it combined with the hueristics.

replies.author: Scott Northwolf
replies.content: but this is just a snapshot of an already advanced stage on a bigger process, you'll find that there are missing components you'd need to understand in depth to do this in such a way, when I did that my community grew from 10 members to around 50 in one month organically and quite easily, but the best part is that those members were exactly my dream customer avatar so conversion followed for my high ticket offer.

replies.content: @Scott Northwolf thanks for the tips!

replies.content: @Scott Northwolf ofcourse, I only need to be level 5 it seems to message you. you can pm me on instagram if you want.
