original_post.author: Piotr Rzepecki
original_post.title: Why Most People Don’t Get Results (Simple Fix)
original_post.body: Most people don’t fail from lack of knowledge. They fail from lack of action.  Facts: 👉 70-90% of people never finish courses. 👉 Most never launch anything. 👉 First attempt is usually bad, but necessary.  What actually works: 1. Launch fast First version in 24-72h  2. Talk to people Send 20 messages Goal is 3-5 real responses  3. Post daily Even simple posts build momentum  4. Improve based on feedback Not on your own ideas  5. Repeat Every week, not once  That’s it No results = no volume More action = more feedback = better results Start now Fix later

original_post.author: Cris Morales
original_post.title: Networking Fatigue?
original_post.body: I'm all in the strategy of comments, chats, posts, dms, etc. But I do sometimes go to make my daily contributions in a group, and end up leaving a "congrats man!". Sometimes, there is nothing else to say.  Sometimes, groups aren't super active, or they aren't talking about what I know I can help them with. My question to myself and to you guys is  If there is nothing to say, should we even say it? Or should we ALWAYS be making ourselves visible, chatting, commenting, posting in the group, even though it doesn't have much to do with your niche, offer, solution, etc?

comment.author: Lexi Cruz
comment.content: Good question! I think the goal isn’t just visibility, it’s contribution. If there’s nothing meaningful to add, forcing a comment can start to feel transactional. When you genuinely help someone or add a thoughtful perspective, that’s when people actually remember you.That’s also why Synthesizer has standards around comment quality. If everyone posted low-effort comments just to stay visible, it would change the energy of the community. I'm curious how others think about that balance.

replies.author: Deekshak Dk
replies.content: @Lexi Cruz true. been frustrated actually with the comments in the community which are just made out of pure transactional intent. Like there's no fricking point in making those "LFG" or those type of comments, yet most here (including me when I started commenting) are just overly enthusiastic and pretending to be giving a shit, whereas they just care about leaving comments mindlessly, just for the sake of it. You felt that? I am guilty of it cuz I did that too, I'm not perfect, and then I felt its not worth putting a mask, so quit that bad habit.

comment.author: Thomas El Nadir
comment.content: @Cris Morales I can definitely relate, and even made a post today in Synthesizer about how I struggled with it last week and the solution I found that made a real difference.In short I'd say that nothing is more important than high leverage work. Everything that isn't should be reconsidered, postponed or simply removed. To leave more place for it.Here's some strategies I implemented in that situation:Reduce the amount of time I spend doing it. At first I started with The 100, and after 30mn I started with the low quality engagement comments. Same with posts and chats. So I reduced "The 100" to "The 30". With that I'm sure that in my allocated time I'll focus on quality, I feel satisfied to have reach my goal, and more often than not I end up doing more than planned because I enter that flow state of "it's just bonus time so I'll only do what I feel excited about anyway". Therefore it's much more productive and impactful. It's repeated many times in the roadmap and in "The 100": quality > quantity. Always.Choose what I comment on. If I don't feel concerned, if I'm not moved or if I don't care about a post or comment, then I don't engage with it. There's plenty of people that leave insightful posts and comments that make my brain and feelings work, so then it's easy to just express myself and connect deeper. But these posts and comments are hidden between less meaningful ones (to me), so I just ignore them because if I feel that way I know my contributions wouldn't matter anyway, for either of us.

replies.author: Deekshak Dk
replies.content: @Thomas El Nadir I agree. Consistency always comes before Volume. Personally experienced this myself when I try to do "more" before having built the capacity to even move past the resistance of starting that activity, I fall into the all-or-nothing pattern, where I end up not doing the thing at all. It happens because of wrong expectations with our work ethic.

replies.author: Cris Morales
replies.content: @Thomas El Nadir I love this approach. Because like I said, I do sometimes feel like I do comments that really aren't moving, or impactful, and I'd much rather scour my groups until I find a comment or post that TRULY I can help with (like the on you just did), and then comment.Thanks for the detailed comment Thomas!

replies.author: Thomas El Nadir
replies.content: @Cris Morales My pleasure man, let's move the world one impactful comment at a time :)

replies.author: Cris Morales
replies.content: @Thomas El Nadir I'm in!

comment.content: Yeah, might be a controversial take mostly on skool, but I generally believe like if you have nothing useful to say don't say it. I want every comment someone reads from me to feel high quality, thoughtful and intentional. Not just a low quality something, because every message you leave signals to people what type of consultant creator and synthesizer you are.

comment.author: Harry Montford
comment.content: I never try to force a comment since people can see through it but also it doesn't provide value in any way in my opinion. Some posts demand me to make a comment that takes 5 minutes to write because it generates so many ideas, sometimes it takes a few seconds depending on the post that was written. But my golden rule is: never force a comment (and that goes for posts as well).

replies.author: Cris Morales
replies.content: @Harry Montford love that idea man. And not only never force, but if it's not going to be a good one, than don't comment it

comment.author: Ryan Grant
comment.content: As others say:Conserve energy Put that energy towards something meaningful you have to sayBut I also like to look at it like:You don't want to think of it like something you 'have' to do. As soon as you develop that mindset it will become like a task, chore, and it won't be meaningful.A great example of this is when I used to 'gratitude journal' I used to sit at my desk everyday and used to think about 3 things I was grateful for everyday. The concept is fantastic, but it turned into something I 'had' to do not what I 'wanted' to do. And I ended up saying the most stupid, mindless stuff for the sake of it and it didn't provide me any benefit at all...🤣Same goes here with commenting and networking. If you feel like you have to just for the sake of it, it won't be of benefit to you and you probably won't reap the rewards.Also think of it like going to a convention in real life....If you 'had' to speak to 15 different people compared to 'wanting' to speak to people, make new connections....you're going to be more mindful, excited to speak to others therefore you're actually going there with a deeper intention which will help you curate the connections you need and actually have meaningful conversations.I've probs gone too far with the points here but I hope that makes sense :)

replies.author: Cris Morales
replies.content: @Ryan Grant No it for sure makes sense! Not making it a chore, but actually wanting to have meaningful conversations with people, which leads to valuable comments like the one you just created!

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content: Less is more.You cannot pour from an empty cup.Fill up your cup.Go read. Learn something new. Meditate. Workout. Stay in silence for a day or two and stop doing outreach (I know it sounds counterproductive but it's what has worked for me).Then you go back only when you have something to say, after a day or two you will...And then magic will happen.Remember that you don't have to do this... you GET to do this! And that's a beautiful priviledge that we have. If we are not having fun and enjoying it, and feeling exactly how we would feel on a 9 to 5 or something like that... then what's the point?Like Nietzsche said "A man can bear almost any HOW as long as he has a WHY."You must see the point in this, otherwise you'll burnout.But sometimes pushing to our limits and actually trying hard (especially to build discipline with regards to outreach and networking) will give you also the beautiful opportunity to experience that sweet contrast between the two approaches and as usual... the most effective way lies somewhere in the middle, when you strike that perfect balance and fluctuate between the two.Life is rhythmic and trying to force reality into one stale structure will always result in dissapointment.Detatch yourself from expectations and learn to enjoy the journey.I know it sounds like a cliche but it has been said by so many wise men for a reason.It is true.I hope this helps, brother.

replies.author: Cris Morales
replies.content: @Scott Northwolf Amazing comment man thank you so much for taking the time to write this out!Yes, I understand. Sometimes, going and doing stuff to be able to talk about it is what is going to make networking, commenting, and coming up with content easy! I also understand what you mean by letting that "white noise" come into play, so I can let my brain rest and recharge with new ideasThanks again!

replies.content: @Cris Morales you're welcome, brother!

comment.author: Mark Santos
comment.content: (edited)I believe there is a huge power in networking and connecting with people who aren't in our niche.It's not always about converting the person we're talking to, but there's a compounding result to having a bunch of people know about you and what you do, because they can refer you.This is a mistake I've made in previous businesses I wanted to build.I was always doing it "by myself" all because I was ashamed of putting myself out there.But the more people know about you and the better the association they have with you, the higher your chances are of people speaking about you to others.And when people have a problem you can solve, they'll refer you over anyone else.That's the power of networking!It may be a bit slower, and a bit boring but with everything.Consistent actions lead to comounding result!@Cris Morales

replies.author: Cris Morales
replies.content: @Mark Santos Man this is amazing, because I actually am going through something similar IRL, in the social side of things, where just knowing people in my city is really powerful.I guess I'll take that mindset into networking here for my business! thanks Mark!

replies.author: Mark Santos
replies.content: @Cris Morales Let's goo!Just to add a funny little story of a cat I met the other day that ties into networking.I visited some friends of my family and they had this beautiful cat. Everybody has been saying that this cat is the sweetest cat ever.The thing is.The cat was just chilling and you could pet him and he was very social with everyone.Just the fact that he didn't shy away from any one meant that he deserved the status of "the sweetest cat ever".The point I wanna make is when people see you around, and you got a good vibe around of you, people will associate that with you.And with us humans, this plays a very big part when it comes to doing business with anyone.We want to work with awesome people.Not bitter, anti-social business owners.And the only way for people to find our whether you're an awesome guy who does X thing, is through networking

replies.author: Cris Morales
replies.content: @Mark Santos very good point (and great story). just being nice, and putting yourself out there is super powerful

original_post.author: Harry Montford
original_post.title: What is your ideal "deep work archetype"?
original_post.body: We have 4 main deep work archetypes.  These are like the 4 classes you can pick inside of a video game. Each have their own benefits, buffs and weaknesses.  They are: - Monastic: 1 main task that you work on for most of the day, minimal breaks. - Bidomal: Long deep work days, but also complete rest days inside the week.  - Rhythmic: Deep work sessions in the morning, shallow work in the afternoon -> evening.  - Journalistic: No clear structure, one minute you could be partying in Bali, the next you are recording          a YT video.  My personal favourite is Rhythmic, but I also know many people who prefer the Monastic work type.  SO here's my question, what would do you follow and which one would you LIKE to try?

original_post.author: J'son Elizondo
original_post.title: The Rise of AI Slop (And the Death of Real Synthesizers)
original_post.body:
𝗔𝗶 𝗦𝗹𝗼𝗽.. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗗𝗲𝗮𝗱 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗲𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗼𝗿𝘆 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗦𝘆𝗻𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘀𝗶𝘇𝗲𝗿 𝗣𝗼𝘀𝘁𝘀..
Yeah I said it Synthesizer is being filled with articles written by BOTS! 🤖
Funniest thing I've seen is 2 articles back to back written by two separate people, same message almost the exact wording. (Not funny at all actually)
The information in this place used to be 𝗱𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗲, 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗿𝗮𝘄..
More value per syllable than any other community..
But now its being littered with em dashes, regurgitated nonsense and oh yea my favorite one, intellectual parroting (sarcasm).
The dead internet theory is a concept predicting that the internet will become predominantly composed of AI-generated content and bot activity, rather than human interaction..
The sad part is that WE are spreading this ai slop. (Literally doing the bid of AI).
By increasing the pace at which it swallows, LITERALLY engulfs the internet whole.
We are masquerading.
Devaluing our own authenticity.
Impersonating what it's like to be a human.
I want to share that community is built on connection and connection is real and raw.
And RAW makes mistakes. Those mistakes in the moment to you, might seem like flaws or blemishes that should be covered. But I want to argue that it is those blemishes are what will distinguish YOU from all of the other articles, posts and NOW 𝗔𝗜- 𝗚𝗘𝗡𝗘𝗥𝗔𝗧𝗘𝗗 𝗦𝗟𝗢𝗣 𝗣𝗥𝗢𝗚𝗥𝗔𝗠𝗦.. <-- (Can you believe that ?!)
⭐Studies now show that we even speak like ai ⭐
Tell me if you've heard this.
"Not only is this not x, But its Y"
"Its not x but Y"
And words like DELVE 🤣😆😂
The Rule of Threes. 𝗘𝘅. ( Clear, concise, and compelling) (I DONT even feel right using the rule of threes in formulating my writing because I think people will perceive it as AI. (Even though I speak like that naturally as a philosophy reader)
Its naturally rolling off of our tongues at this point because we consume it SO OFTEN.
ONLY increasing the very PROBLEM that this community aims to solve in the first place..
🌊"𝗪𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗱𝗿𝗼𝘄𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝗻 𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻, 𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘄𝗶𝘀𝗱𝗼𝗺"🌊
With every article generated by ai you are effectively adding MORE water to the already filled bucket.
I want to share with you that not polishing an article, post or comments is actually going to 𝗵𝗲𝗹𝗽 𝘆𝗼𝘂, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝘆𝗼𝘂.
🤮Articles on social media migration and social media rebellion states: "THEY ARE LEAVING BECAUSE IT NO LONGER 𝗙𝗘𝗘𝗟𝗦 𝗥𝗘𝗔𝗟" SAYING IT 𝗙𝗘𝗘𝗟𝗦 𝗔𝗥𝗧𝗜𝗙𝗜𝗖𝗜𝗔𝗟, 𝗨𝗡𝗥𝗘𝗔𝗟 𝗔𝗡𝗗 𝗠𝗔𝗡𝗜𝗣𝗨𝗟𝗔𝗧𝗘𝗗.. "IT FEELS LIKE A 𝗙𝗔𝗞𝗘 𝗘𝗡𝗩𝗜𝗢𝗥𝗡𝗠𝗘𝗡𝗧 𝗖𝗥𝗘𝗔𝗧𝗘𝗗 𝗕𝗬 𝗔 𝗠𝗔𝗖𝗛𝗜𝗡𝗘" ..🤮
So with all that in mind..
Think before you use ai.
Think before you let it write your articles for you.
And for god sakes don't let it write your whole program from conception to birth, so you can COLD pitch it to someone FOR money.
𝗧𝗛𝗔𝗧 𝗶𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗯𝗲𝗮𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗳𝘂𝗹 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘆. 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝗰𝗮𝗺.
A NOT so clever scam that aims to take advantage of people who are in pain and need experts, 𝗥𝗘𝗔𝗟 𝗦𝗬𝗡𝗧𝗛𝗘𝗦𝗜𝗭𝗘𝗥𝗦, 𝗡𝗢𝗧 𝗔𝗜 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗽𝘁 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹𝘀.
Authenticity over curation
Authenticity over curation
say it with me now
Authenticity over curation
"𝗪𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗱𝗿𝗼𝘄𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝗻 𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻, 𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘄𝗶𝘀𝗱𝗼𝗺"

comment.author: Nani Wright
comment.content:
Well said. I think the problem is that people are using it for everything. It's fine to use it, it reduces friction, lowers the learning curve, makes it easier to just start. The problem is when you default to it for literally everything and outsource your entire brain to it. At that point you're going backwards, steadily losing the ability to think critically and make decisions for yourself.
I read the other day that military LEADERS are using it to help make strategic decisions 😅. So yeah, It's here and it's not going anywhere. We might as well learn to love it. At the same time, authenticity is going to stick out like a sore thumb now. There's a real opportunity in just being more human.

comment.author: Elias Thorne
comment.content:
@J'son Elizondo, I'm pretty new here but I appreciate this post. I am just starting to poke around and learn about the Synthesizer community and was curious about how much of the content is AI generated.
I do not have a problem with AI generated content, as long as it solves a problem for me in a way that I could not do myself. More content is not necessarily better.
I think part of the problem is that people may be going to AI to get started before they have a clear idea of what they want to do, and how they can best do whatever that is.
I view AI as an accelerator for an already good idea, initiative, etc. Without a good starting point, AI just amplifies what already exists, or worst case, aids in masking the fact that there is no new creativity behind the words.

comment.author: Des Cooke
comment.content:
I have a close contact who is a content writer, a ghost writer essentially. One of the things the ghostwriting community has been saying for a very long time is that AI will never replace them because of the fact that you can witness ai content, as you say you can see it.
One of the recent developments is that that person has now got an AI machine essentially that's taken time to build.
That machine is trained on lots of a person's content, like:
it's trained on their videos
it's trained on their podcasts
it's trained on their blog posts, etc.
They can literally talk into their phone about a story, an idea, a thought, a meeting. "I've just had a conversation with the director about blah blah blah blah blah, and by the time they get back to the desk, there's a post already written that takes almost no editing!
It is now spitting out posts that are relevant to the arena the person works in, providing it's trained enough and that the input posts are actually good enough.
Someone like me has decades of back catalogue.
That ghost writer is moving f̲r̲o̲m̲ ghost writing,
with the community of people in ghost writing saying "AI will never replace them",
t̲o̲ delivering people access to this server that can be trained on their back catalogue so that it can replace their posts within the content that they actually have.
I suppose this is a bit like using Notebook LM, etc., but this seems to be much more advanced, and I've seen some of the output.
Initially, he was completely distraught and downhearted that he'd built himself out of a job, but this would actually be a product with the hundreds of files put together and the person accessing it remotely linking their own content for it to be trained on. Essentially, a cost to set it up, and be trained how to use it and train it - then the customer could run it on their own machine.
It's actually as good as he can do now as a ghostwriter and is indistinguishable from his own written content, even by someone like him who notices the normal AI slop a lot.
As Ferris Bueller said: "𝘓𝘪𝘧𝘦 𝘮𝘰𝘷𝘦𝘴 𝘱𝘳𝘦𝘵𝘵𝘺 𝘧𝘢𝘴𝘵. 𝘐𝘧 𝘺𝘰𝘶 𝘥𝘰𝘯'𝘵 𝘴𝘵𝘰𝘱 𝘢𝘯𝘥 𝘭𝘰𝘰𝘬 𝘢𝘳𝘰𝘶𝘯𝘥 𝘰𝘯𝘤𝘦 𝘪𝘯 𝘢 𝘸𝘩𝘪𝘭𝘦, 𝘺𝘰𝘶 𝘤𝘰𝘶𝘭𝘥 𝘮𝘪𝘴𝘴 𝘪𝘵."
It'll be a matter of weeks, possibly months, before the world has caught up with him. And you won't be able to tell?
Question: Did I write this with AI?

comment.author: Lexi Cruz
comment.content:
This is definitely real. People naturally look for what works and try to replicate it, that’s always been there. What’s changed is how easy it is now. Before, you had to understand something at least a little to repeat it. Now you can assemble something that sounds right without actually going through it.
It’s kind of funny too, because AI is trained on human content. It’s basically like a copy of a copy of a copy at this point. So it feels less like AI vs authenticity, and more like the gap between what someone has actually experienced and what they can now simulate. That’s usually what feels off. At this point it comes down to how much discernment the person using it has, not the tool itself.
And if anything, it’s making real, lived insight stand out more.

comment.author: Selene Chaves
comment.content:
I love this post, and funnily enough, I'm dictating this reply to Claude. Why? Because I have a tendency to repeat myself, and I find it useful to use it as my assistant to make MY message clear.
I don't see things in black and white, and I do see the real benefit of AI. I use it as an assistant to simplify my life and make things faster, not to do the work for me or replace my voice.
I'm very busy, and using it to speed things up rather than replace my thinking is genuinely an amazing tool. But that's where it becomes a huge problem: when we STOP thinking altogether and let it do everything for us.
So let's use these tools wisely. Let's stay HUMAN❤️‍🔥
Thank you, @J'son Elizondo, for the amazing post.

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content:
Bro, I agree completely. I am a writter at heart and you and I have this beautiful obsession with knowledge and understanding, and I know you draw as much satisfaction from teaching and sharing with others your knowledge as I do... and it is quite literally like a drug for us... but in a good sense, because we get to connect very deeply with amazing people and we get to touch their lives in very impactful ways changing it for the better.
I'm really glad you are bringing this to people's attention because this is something I ALWAYS say to people: If you let AI write for you... I mean... can you really take pride on what "you wrote"? It's not even your own knowledge... and it's sad... because by teaching we solidify our knowledge.... by teaching we learn at the same time.
This is what we are here for and letting something as beautiful as that die would be a true shame.
At the same time... I don't think the AI slop is so bad. It's actually WONDERFUL! Because the more of it there is out there the more we will stand out for being authentically ourseleves and the more rare we'll be. We'll be the only ones willing to do the "hard work" of writting and teaching ourselves and we will benefit greatly personally from that.
The work works more on you than you work on it, and that's a fact, so when people chose the easy path, the path of least resistance they are missing out on the amazing gift that focus and deep work bring to their lives, the development of their discipline, the development of their capacity to actually sit in presence on the present moment and give their best to something beautiful and meaningful like it is connecting with people and helping them change their lives.
I truly loved this post. Thank you so much for sharing it with me, brother... and above all: thank you for being you, Jason, you are a shinning lighthouse in the middle of this dark sea and you have no idea of how deeply appreciated you are, man.
Delusion backed up just by words it's just delusion, but delusion backed up by relentless action... that's called FAITH, brother! Remember: Duty, Honor and Pride!!!

comment.author: Ben Sherry
comment.content:
This is SUCH an interesting take 🤖📊 and honestly… a little ironic (in the best way) 😅
Because what we’re actually seeing isn’t just “AI taking over”…
It’s a feedback loop 🔁Humans read AI ✍️ → Humans sound like AI 🗣️ → Humans blame AI 🤖
Not only is AI shaping content… it’s shaping how we THINK 🧠💭
You mentioned patterns like:“Not X… but Y”Rule of 3️⃣Overly clean phrasing
That’s not random. That’s optimization 📈Language is being refined into what is:
Clear ✅
Efficient ⚡
Widely understood 🌍
But here’s the real question… ❓👉 If everything starts to sound the same… what actually stands out?
Answer:
🔥 Imperfection
🔥 Messiness
🔥 Real, unfiltered thought
Because when EVERYTHING is polished… polish becomes invisible 👀
So authenticity doesn’t die here… it actually becomes MORE valuable 💎
In a world of infinite “perfect” content…👉 The rough edge becomes the signal👉 The human glitch becomes the hook
So yeah… should we stop using AI? ❓No.
We should stop outsourcing our voice 🎤
Use AI for structure 🧱
But keep the chaos 😈
Use AI for clarity 🔍
But keep the contradictions 🤯
Because THAT’S what feels real.
And if everything becomes optimized, predictable, and clean…
Then the most human thing you can do is break the pattern 💥

original_post.author: Lea Newkirk
original_post.title: The "Learning" Loop 🔄
original_post.body:
For a long time, I was addicted to the feeling of potential.
I’d spend hours consuming manifestation videos and different types of self-improvement techniques, convinced I was making progress because I understood the concepts. But my life wasn't actually moving. My mind was racing ahead while my physical reality was standing still. I realized I was using "learning" as a safety blanket. It was a way to feel productive without ever having to risk the discomfort of actually putting myself out there. I was feeding my brain, but starving my results. I found that I didn’t actually need more videos or books. I needed to take the information out of your head and move it into my daily life.
I’m curious about your experience:
Have you ever used "researching" as a way to avoid the actual work?
What’s one action you’re taking today that doesn't involve a screen?
Would love to see where this discussion goes ✨

comment.author: Germain-Blaise Nkede
comment.content: This is real because a lot of us have been there, feeling productive just from learning but not actually moving anything forward. I’ve definitely caught myself doing that, especially when something feels uncomfortable or uncertain, it’s easier to watch another video than take action. What helped me shift was realizing clarity doesn’t come from more information, it comes from doing and adjusting as you go. Lately I’ve been focusing on taking at least one action a day that actually moves something in real life, even if it’s small, because that builds momentum way faster than just thinking about it. Today for me that’s getting a solid workout in and knocking out something I’ve been putting off instead of overplanning it 💪🏾

comment.author: Lukas Schmidt

Here is a valuable reframe I learned.

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content:
It's not a loop... it's a spiral...
It keeps progressing over time.
We flood our unconscious mind with knowledge until the unconscious surface to the conscious.
This is a good insight but there is no right or wrong approach, there's just different approaches depending on your ultimate goal.
Do you want to get results quicker?
Apply the scientific method.
Acquire enough knowledge so you can formulate a theory.
Grab that theory and go out there and test it.
Track the data you get out of it.
Analayze the feedback and readjust accordingly for the next reiteration to improve.
There are 3 components to the META-skill, they are:
1-Deliberate practice.
2-Reflection upon your practice.
3-Theoretical knowledge applied to your practice.
Applying correctly and consistently these 3 components you'll be able to gain mastery over ANY skill that you desire over time.
All you need to be sucessful is: Clarity (at least enough so you can take action intentionally), Commitment (a firm decision to follow through until you hit your goal) and consistency.
1-Set a goal and craft a theory with a minimum amount of critical knowledge.
2-Commit to such goal so you know you'll hit it sooner or later and decide not to stop until you do so.
3-Take action relentlessly to hit such goal and pay attention to the feedback by simply tracking what you are doing, analyzing your intentionality and understanding if it's beeing fulfilled or not and why so you can reiterate and improve your theory with such data.

original_post.author: Kasparas Stancikas
original_post.title: 🥊 First Ever Kickboxing Fight... (Knockout, Faked Documents, Work Ethic)
original_post.body:
Just before i tell you about the craziest 30 days of my life, i want to thank every single one of you for keeping me accountable under my daily accountability posts:)))
Thanks Synthesizers 🤝
THE STORY:
34 days ago my great friend told me:
"Kasparas, in a month there’s this amateur kickboxing competition."
2 seconds later, I said NO because right now i am:
Following synthesizer roadmap and thats my nr1 priority in life
i want to put all my focus into my business
Its too early I’ve only been training for 1 year
Excuses...
But as I continued with my day, this thought stuck in my head. At the end of the day i always had a dream to compete in atleast 1 martial arts fight
My gut intuition was getting stronger and stronger...
So I DMed my trainer to ask if I was even qualified to compete.
I got a response: “Only if you promise to train really damn hard.”
And that’s where it all started...
I quit bodybuilding and went all in on martial arts training 1-2 times a day and keeping myself accountable in synthesizer every single day for 30 days straight sharing how i am prepping for my fight + scaling my business all at the same time
There was one issue tho...
I still had no clue how would I get my parents to sign the agreement letting me fight? (i am 17yo)
My mom is really against fighting, she HATES it. My dad kind of does too. So, two weeks into training, when it was time to sign the papers, I did what any normal guy would do...
I faked the documents:D I forged my dad’s signature and everything.
My trainer had no clue, and it all worked out.
So 30 days later, after an insane amount of training and dieting (and still horrible cardio), I was about to step into the ring with another 81kg guy who was a year older and had 3 years of training to rip my head off
Round 1: Went really damn well. I was physically stronger, so I scared him away with some jabs and leg kicks.
Round 2: Still going decent, but I started getting extremely gassed out at the end.
Round 3: I had zero cardio left, luckily my opponent was hella gassed out too. This round was just to see who could survive the longest. And guess what? With only 10 seconds left, I landed a clear hook and KNOCKED HIM OUT, winning the god damn fight!
I still remember my friends screaming in the corner. (Watch the vid below.)
That was one of the BEST moments of my life...
I grew so much as a person, gained so much wisdom, and even managet to CLOSE MULTIPLE HIGH TICKET deals during the prep.
I proved to myself that ANYTHING is possible. If you really want it bad enough you can scale your business and chase your other wild dreams all at the same time
No excuses are valid.
I had to hide from my family, prepare for exams and run a business all at the same time...
if i can do it you can too
What is that dream you been pushing for later? (tell me in the comments)
0:26

original_post.title: What stands in the way becomes the way.
original_post.body:
Been reading the book of Marcus Aurelius. I've learned:
Look at obstacles as a learning opportunity.
Intentionally find obstacles.
Embrace obstacles.
And as a synthesizer you can just replace "obstacles" with "calls", go and read that again 😂
What's your perspective on the obstacles in your journey?
Ps: Attached an image from Andrew Kirby ( Being Cringe = Obstacle )

comment.author: Kirti Gilbert
comment.content: This keeps any hint of victim mentality at bay. Victim thinking is abundant, and its a tactic to keep us sitting on the other side unable to take hold of the prize which is set before us, to which we are called. So I take hold of the call and I run with perseverance the race marked out for me - thank you for sharing.

@Kirti Gilbert Exactly. I don't believe in victim mentality because:
We want the best for ourselves.
It's just our own selves trying to keep us from improving.
The best way to fight it is just acknowledging it.
I love how you mentioned just running with perseverance!
Where are you right now in your synthesizer journey?

original_post.author: Tom Stoll
original_post.title: sell what they want, give what they need
original_post.body:
would you agree?
yeas
no (why in comments)
other (also in comments)
27 votes

comment.author: Neil Abbott
comment.content:
I tend to have the beliefs of switching that statement up... giving what they want; selling what they need.
It's a bit like the $100M series of books Hormozi preaches on. Give so much value up front that it's difficult to say no to the offer...

replies.author: Andrew Ng
replies.content: @Neil Abbott its the same with this community, so much value for free here

replies.author: Michael Pluszek
replies.content: @Neil Abbott this makes sense in many ways - I have an idea, that because there is so much value online for free now, incl. how Skool communities kind of growth this exponential - The reason someone will buy - Will be different than a few years ago - Makes sense?

replies.author: Neil Abbott
replies.content:
@Michael Pluszek ... you're spot on. Doing the math of these claims serves a bit like Astronomy though... where we have to use trigonometry to guess that where we want to land is where we think when the ship arrives.
If that math becomes known, be sure to let me know!

replies.author: Michael Pluszek
replies.content: @Neil Abbott 🙂 that's an interesting perspective.

replies.author: Tom Stoll
replies.content:
@Neil Abbott
That's a really interesting perspective though.
When you give them something that they want, isn't that selling?
When you sell something that they need, they don't necessarily want that so when you sell them you didn't convince them, right?

replies.author: Brandon Smith
replies.content:
@Neil Abbott I think what Tom means here in a marketing perspective, is that you want to lean towards their interest in making a proper buying decision to solve their problems, so that when they're sold, you can give them what they need.
For example: Say your product delivery is about identity-shifting and mindset, but you're marketing towards Soccer players who are looking to improve their shooting.
Would you sell them on the identity-shifting, or sell them on improving their shooting first? I'd argue the latter.
You can begin starting to work on their identity-shifting/mindset when they're bought in (if you know that's what's going to help them get results, to be clear)
Does that make sense Neil?

replies.author: Neil Abbott
replies.content:
(edited)
@Tom Stoll ... absolutely, giving them what they want is selling. Just more more along the lines of serving as a value add and the client, than a service or product from the pitch deck.
In the end, its the buyer/client that determines the value... both of what's given and sold. Wants and needs are also something that is deternined by the buyer/client.
Scoping this debate from the perspective of the seller/giver should live as a secondary measure at best... the client/buyer always comes first.

replies.author: Neil Abbott
Neil Abbott
replies.content:
@Brandon Smith ... sure, I don't ride on the laurels that what I'm saying is cut & dry always the right way to go about sales. I'm quick to feel that needs are water, food, & shelter type of things... wants are kicking a soccer ball or the identity in doing so...
I keep a strong focus on the why, then the what, and lastly the how.

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content:
I agree completely.
First thing to understand is where people are amongst the 3 awareness levels.
Are they?
1-No awareness (they are in pain but don't know what's their problem).
2-Problem aware (they know they are in pain and know at least on a high level view what's their problem and starting to activeley look for solutions).
3-Solution awareness (know they have a problem and know what's the solution, now they are ready to take action on it).
Level 2 and 3 are the best parts.
That's the warmest leads you'll get.
Funnel structure:
1-At level one you call out their pain and attract them to your community (or YT channel or whatever).
2-At level 2 you get them inside your community and give them declarative knowledge so they start seeing what's their "enemy", their problem.
3-At level 3 you present them with the solution and they are ready to start taking actionable steps in order to solve it, usually this is best done on a sales' call already.
Delusion backed up just by words it's just delusion but delusion backed up by relentless action... that's called FAITH, brother! Remember: Duty, Honor and Pride!!!

comment.author: Marvin Brito
comment.content: Give what they want first. Later on, they will realize they actually need it. They just didn't know they needed it.

original_post.author: Lukas Schmidt
original_post.title: I lost the Skool Games. Don't repeat my mistakes
original_post.body:
Since I joined Skool back in 2024, I wanted to win the Skool Games.
It is the closest thing to the business Olympics there is.
But that also means you compete against the best.
The best have a massive audience with teams and systems in place to scale a low-ticket offer.
(In the Skool Games, you can charge a maximum of $100 per month or $1200 per year)
I worked a lot more in 26 Q1 compared to 25 Q4, but made a lot less.
Still grateful for the experience and had a lot of fun.
But I wouldn't recommend it to anyone who is getting started.
In the beginning, it is much more effective to charge high ticket and scale as much as possible.
Instead of chasing the shiny trophy, I am committed to building a solid foundation.
I also found it to be much easier to help someone else win.
Congrats again to @Michael Rizk and @Jenna Kelly.
You guys crushed it.
I am also excited to be more active here again, since I am not locked into the competition.
Let's change the world together.
What are you focused on in Q2?

What is the goal of your app?

replies.author: Obatero Jones
replies.content:
@Lukas Schmidt The goal of my app is give the public a better way to map their patterns, and align with success.
Everybody has energy they are born with, and spend years trying to figure out what their purpose is. I easily map it out for you, your business, show patterns, and cycles. When to make money, when to build, when to expand. I answer those questions easily. The app is the visual that gives the map, I help users actually activate the energies within the map. Call it Divine timing, or just pattern mapping, either way it gives you insight you can not get anywhere else.

replies.author: Easton Delaney
replies.content: @Obatero Jones I love how you’re thinking and how you’re so clear on the direction you're headed it's definitely not gibberish! It sounds like you’re cultivating something really special, both for yourself and your community. Focusing on the growth of your community, even with low engagement in the beginning, is a wise move. You’re absolutely right: when you build it with intention, the right people will find their way to it, and that’s where the magic happens. Your idea of moving into case studies of prominent figures and mapping patterns to foresee future possibilities is so unique and powerful. There’s so much potential in learning from history and using that to guide the future. I can totally see how that could be both inspiring and enlightening for your community. And the fact that your app is already receiving good reviews? That’s amazing progress! As you continue to innovate your thinking and integrate all these pieces, I’m curious: What kind of impact do you hope to create for the people in your community? What’s the deeper vision driving all of this?

replies.author: Obatero Jones
replies.content: @Easton Delaney tbh, the deeper vision driving all of this is never feeling like I have a place in the world. I have always felt left out, or behind, or even just isolated because of how/who I am. I found that my style of thinking most of the time is AHEAD of the time, so its hard to stay socially aware, I went into my spiritual journey looking for acceptance, and found more rejection. I found my ideas are ahead of time and people look at me crazy when I have my ideas. I hope that I can bring numerologists, astrologers, and even those that stude chinese and Eastern cosmology together with one cohesive system. We are all correct, we just only have pieces to the puzzle. I want to unite us, and help bring a deeper understanding as to why we are here. I know its oblong, but I also know that it will catch on. Like I said im always early with my ideas... this time though, instead of giving the idea away and feeling like I stabbed myself later, I cultivated this, me and God. He will have the Glory, excuse me but I have to give reverence to where the vision, and idea. Its greater than me, and its helped me to accept who I truly am, my contradictions and my patterns. Now all I can do is position myself correctly, and watch everything unfold. Thank you for asking! 😊

replies.author: Easton Delaney
replies.content: @Obatero Jones Wow, thank you for sharing that with me it’s so deeply powerful. I can really feel the weight and the beauty of your vision, and I admire your courage to embrace it despite the challenges you’ve faced. Feeling like you’ve never quite had a place in the world is something that many of us can relate to, especially when our thoughts and ideas feel ahead of their time. But the fact that you’re not only seeing a way forward but are actively creating that space is incredible. It’s like you’re building the bridge for others who might be walking the same path as you, feeling isolated or misunderstood. I love that you're bringing together numerologists, astrologers, and those who study Eastern cosmology. You're absolutely right: we all hold pieces of the puzzle, and your ability to see that and connect them is a truly rare gift. The way you’ve found acceptance not through external validation but through a deeper understanding of yourself and your relationship with God is so inspiring. It’s clear that this journey is not just about building a community, but about creating a new understanding for all of us a vision that’s bigger than just you, and that’s so special. I’m curious, though how has your journey been so far in terms of growth and self-discovery? What kind of breakthroughs have you had along the way, especially in terms of aligning your inner world with your outer vision?

replies.author: Easton Delaney
replies.content: @Obatero Jones Hello

replies.author: Obatero Jones
replies.content: @Easton Delaney oh that’s a doozy, I relocated in 2024, to Dallas from Miami, where I started my spiritual journey. I will try not to ramble lol, but 2025 was a monumental year for me, my father died in January, and the grieving process really shows you yourself, as well as others. I can say I discovered how my father’s family actually saw me(their version). I went from that to seeing that I was looked at as a slave in my job, and when I started to speak up, being an independent contractor, that showed me how my job sees me. I guess you can say the year of the snake was like cutting the grass, burning the shrubs, and watching my life implode because it was built on a false narrative. 2025 caused a lot of introspection turning inward. If I’m being transparent, I say with myself and admitted to myself that I played the fool for so long that I actually turned into one. I’ve dumbed down my influence so long you could say imposter syndrome became my home, and my safe space. So, that was my “aha” moment, it’s so much easier to get involved and help someone build an empire, I’ve done it. I have the credibility for it, but I always wanted to be behind the scenes, but with me creating this system and seeing everything in one place I realized I am supposed to be in front. My own blueprint gave me such insight that I looked at myself like WHOA! I created something that even threw me for a loop. It took the information I had already gathered about myself and amplified patterns, which gave me a chance to pivot into my purpose. I will always be grateful for introspection, my faith, and the ability for God like visions to come through, and that I am actually capable of accomplishing it! One of the greatest gifts God gives us is the ability to wake up and make today better than yesterday, as long as I wake up, I will push this passion.

original_post.author: Piotr Rzepecki
original_post.title: Why Most People Never Make Real Money Online (And What Actually Changes Everything)
original_post.body:
Most people focus on the wrong things → content, followers, looking professional.
But none of that guarantees money.
Attention ≠ income. System = income.
Right now, most people operate like this:
Post → get views → maybe engagement → no sales
And then they think the problem is reach, algorithm, or niche.
It’s not.
The problem is there’s no structure behind what they’re doing. No path. No sequence. No flow.
Just random actions that don’t connect.
Here’s the shift you need to understand: Most money is NOT made on the first sale
Beginners think: I need one perfect product, I need one perfect offer
Reality is different.
First sale = entry point
Some people even go: Break even or lose money on the first offer → just to get the customer in
Why would anyone do that?
Because of this:
1 purchase → much higher chance of 2nd purchase
And that’s where the real money starts.
Not from convincing strangers once. But from serving people who already trust you.
So instead of thinking: How do I sell this one thing?
You need to think: How do I build a path someone can follow?
A simple structure looks like this:
Attention → content, posts, videos
Capture → something valuable in exchange for contact
Trust → consistency, follow-up, useful content
Offer → clear, relevant solution
More value → upsells, deeper or faster outcomes
That’s the system.
Another important reality:
~7 interactions → before someone buys
So if you post once and expect results → wrong expectationIf you post a few times and quit → you never even gave it a chance
People need to see you multiple times. They need to understand you. They need to trust you.
Trust is built through repetition.
That’s why follow-up matters so much.
Not aggressive selling. Not spamming links.
But showing up consistently, giving value, explaining things clearly, and slowly removing doubts.
Now here’s where most people lose again:
They finally get one sale → and stop there
That’s one of the biggest mistakes.
Because:
Best moment to sell again = right after someone buys
At that moment, trust is highest Resistance is lowest.
That’s why this structure works:
Main offer → upsell → downsell → additional value
Important rule:
Main product must already deliver value on its own.
Upsells are not there to fix it. They are there to enhance it.
Example:
Basic solution → advanced version Slow result → faster result Simple info → deeper system
All optional, all relevant.
Now what if someone says no?
Upsell X → Downsell
Lower price, Simpler version, Less friction
And many people who said no at first → say yes here
Because it feels like a better deal
This is not guesswork. This is based on how people actually buy.
Now zoom out again.
Everything starts with a simple entry point:
Value → attention → relationship
You give something useful. You attract the right people. You start building trust.
You’re no longer chasing random traffic.
You’re building a base:
Audience → trust → repeat buyers
That’s how long-term money is created.
Not from one post. Not from one product.
But from a system that keeps working.
Another mistake people make:
They build once → and never test
They assume everything works.
But small issues break everything:
Broken links, confusing offers, bad flow.
And every mistake = lost money
And you don’t get those people back
That’s why people who get results:
Test everything. Go through the process themselves. Fix problems early Improve step by step.
Because once traffic starts coming in, You need the system to work.
Now compare both approaches:
No system: Post → hope → no results
System: Attention → Trust → Offer → More value
One is random. One is controlled.
And control is everything.
Because when you control the system, You control the outcome.
You know what’s working. You know what’s not. You know what to fix.
And that’s when things start to scale.
So if you feel stuck:
It’s not your content. It’s not your niche. It’s not your potential.
It’s the lack of structure behind what you’re doing.
You don’t need more ideas.
You need a system that turns attention into results.
Build it once. Improve it over time.
And everything changes.

comment.author: Hil Kane
comment.content: so succinctly stated that it is nearly a religious experience

comment.author: Scott Northwolf
comment.content:
I'd expand on this:
"A simple structure looks like this:
Attention → content, posts, videos
Capture → something valuable in exchange for contact
Trust → consistency, follow-up, useful content
Offer → clear, relevant solution
More value → upsells, deeper or faster outcomes
That’s the system."
In the following way:
To actually draw attention consistently instead of posting into the void and hoping it will work or spending a lot of time honing your skills to guess what people like... I personally do this:
Select a niche (a group of people without stating their "problem", at this point I don't really know what their most painful percieved problem is).
Conduct niche research interviews (minimum from 14 to 30 but the more the better, but do not procrastinate by elongating this stage) using a script with questions to figure out what's their biggest percieved problem and their desired outcome (you'll pick up on the niche's language as well, this is important).
Build high ticekt offer with the knowledge that you now possess (you should have picked up on the most recurrent patterns) [you can use Alex Hormozi's framework from his book $100M Offers, that's a very good one for getting started].
Develop the methodology on the back-end of your offer in order to help people attain their desired outcome, if it feels like you don't even know if you'll be able to do it that's good, it means you'll have to grow and learn to be able to deliver consistently.
After selecting the niche properly and finding where they hang out and approaching them to get them on a niche research interview... guess what? You already have the skeleton of your outbound system. You just need to go back to the same place and ask them to jump on a call with you, if possible also gather them in one place like a skool community, an instagram page or an email list (this is where content for nurturing your audience becomes important, not saying that starting with content is a bad approach but this is just how I do it and it works for me).
Create sales' script (test for 30 reiterations minimum to know what are your conversion rates with a big enough data sample and when you make a change test always for 30 reiterations) [Jordan Belfort's The Way of Wolf is an amazing book for getting started creating your own sales' script].
Run structured sales' calls consistently and record them in order to optimize.
Track your KPIs (ABR, SUR, GFR and SCR).
Reverse engineer the numbers to your desired monthly income (I think I'll make a post about that soon).
Ok, so then to summerize:
Select niche.
Run niche research interviews (14 to 30).
Grand slam offer.
Methodology development.
Appointment setting (outbound system).
Sales' script development.
Record and optimize sales' calls.
Track your KPIs.
Reverse engineer your desired monthly income periodically using the KPIs data.

original_post.author: Mohammed Attar
original_post.title: My "Wall of Fame" (And the Systems Behind It)
original_post.body:
I see people celebrate wins with screenshots all the time. But screenshots get buried. They disappear in the feed.
I decided to make my wins permanent. These are three 5-star reviews from my best clients. I framed them and hung them on my wall.
Why? Because I need to remind myself of the standard of work I provide. Every day.
And when I’m on a call, or making a post like this, it’s not just words. It’s physical proof of authority.
The Strategy:
Identify your top 3 wins.
Turn them into tangible, physical objects.
Use them to dominate your environment and build authority.
Your wins deserve to be permanent.
Call to Action: What is one win you’ve had this month that is worth of a frame? Post it below! 👇

original_post.author: Ryan Grant
original_post.title: how do you prep for a killer work day?
original_post.body:
As other synthesizers have pointed out in recent posts...
Making sure that the work we are doing is productive and in alignment with our goals is SO IMPORTANT!
And as people say with fitness & the majority of other things too...
What you do outside the gym is just as important as inside the gym.
Regarding this context:
what you do outside of work can be just as important as you working, because the things you do outside of work can either make or break your next work day.
With that being said...
how do you prime/prep yourself for a KILLER work day?
How do you make sure you are 100% and ready to go?
Is that through any planning/organisation strategies?
Going to the gym? Going on a walk?
Any supplements?
Any meditation/practices?
What's your go to priming routine/practices for a killer work day right now? 👇

comment.author: Harrison Todd
comment.content:
plan the day the night before
make sure I get good sleep
wake up, cold shower, work (on highest leverage task)
go through my deep work checklist before the block to pre-emptively reduce distractions (basically steps that I've designed over time to start a work block with as much focus as possible)
reflect at the end of the day so that I can improve for tomorrow by writing 3+ improvements

replies.author: Till Rettig
Harrison Todd
replies.content: @Till Rettig The improvements are actually for the whole day. The usual kinds of things I write down are either things I planned to do but didn't, things I let distract me, etc. Even if they aren't the most specific, they help me with just understanding and thinking about what areas I can improve and do better in.

One of place where I did notice triggers recently was my 9-5. I often open some random doc there and jot down random thoughts towards the end of the day, instead of working.
I makes me feel kinda bad in the evening, because I think this attitude of avoiding the work subconsciously spills into other areas as well.
I've noticed that I'm more productive in all areas of life when I 100% lock in at work.

comment.author: Till Rettig
comment.content:
The place where I'm most productive is my 9-5 work, and I don't really "prime" myself before starting there.
At this point, I know what to do, I know how to start, and so I just start.
I think what's important for me are the lifestyle-habits that support my work, though:
-Get up at 6 am every morning
-Go to bed at 10pm every evening
-Spend at least 30min outside
-Spend at least 30min exercising
-1h of meditation
It's boring stuff haha, but I always break my own neck when I don't follow through with these things
What does a killer work day look like for you @Ryan Grant ?

replies.author: Rodrigo Osorio Lopez
replies.content:
@Till Rettig Imppresive how you can actually feel the difference at so young age. Kudos to you!
do you do any kind of breathing?

replies.author: Till Rettig
replies.content: @Rodrigo Osorio Lopez Kinda, I try to focus on my breath during meditation

replies.author: Ryan Grant
replies.content:
@Till Rettig Fantastic stuff man! Sometimes the boring stuff is the best way to get things done. And sometimes it's the stuff that's perceived 'boring' can be quite fun haha.
Usually start the day by waking between 6/6:30 - eat my first meal of the day and gather things I need to...
7/7:15 I'm in the gym until around 8
Go back home and drive to university for around 9am
Attend lectures throughout the day, work on my business and university degree
Try and take a break and walk around the campus lake for half an hour to break up the work block
Get back to my house around 8:30-8:45pm
Sleep & Repeat :)
What sort of exercise/fitness discipline are you into my man?

replies.author: Till Rettig
replies.content: @Ryan Grant Pretty simple, daily pushups and pullups + leg training 2x per week to get better at running

comment.author: Rodrigo Osorio Lopez
comment.content:
(edited)
I personally rely on the basic principles: hydration, nutrition, movement, breathing and mindset. I understood some years ago that you may have the perfect checklist, doing the perfect routine and feel like shit. One day I got to the point of forcing my self to do the things because "I can do it" and life became very heavy.
After I understood that by taken care of the principles, my body is going to feel on a peak state and the best part is that is not that hard neither time consuming, for busy people having morning routines >45min is something pretty much impossible to sustain in the long run and keeping something consistenly is probably what brings you the best result

original_post.author: Andrew Kirby
original_post.title: Stop Everything. Watch This.
original_post.body:
@Sam Howe has helped make documentaries for David Attenborough.
Now he just made a Movie about Synthesizer.
It made me cry the first time I watched it. And the second. And the third.
It's a masterpiece.
If you're an educator on skool, this is a must watch.
Click here to watch it on YouTube. It's 5 minutes long.
5:11

original_post.author: Brandon Maloney
original_post.title: Do you use Push or Pull Motivation?
original_post.body:
I saw this podcast by Alex Hormozi and Tony Robbins, and it's always stuck out to me the concept of Push versus Pull motivation.
(You can watch it here: https://youtu.be/xz-ymFOhBAE?si=9jY06iWkMi3vSZd1)
Push motivation is a reuiqrement, and obligation, a "have to" mindset that forces you to use willpower.
Pull motivation is a desire, a want, a purposeful NEED to do the thing, without require as much, or any willpower.
I saw this post by @Aathraey Shrikanth, asking what we would teach our younger selves, and I wouldn't "teach myself anything", but I would 100% want to see what would happen if I told myself to rely on pull instead of push.
(That post is here: https://www.skool.com/synthesizer/what-lesson-would-you-teach-your-younger-self)
What do you guys think about this, what are you running on, and what's your experience with the opposite kind of motivation (if any).

comment.author: Kai Cerar
comment.content: Both but mostly Pull

replies.author: Brandon Maloney
replies.content: @Kai Cerar what’s the thing that pulls you?

comment.content: Pull 100%. I was checking up on a client so often yesterday cuz I almost got addicted to giving advice.

replies.author: Harrison Todd
replies.content: @Aathraey Shrikanth Helping clients out is THE BEST.

replies.author: Brandon Maloney
replies.content: @Aathraey Shrikanth that’s amazing bro, what gives you your pull?

replies.author: Aathraey Shrikanth
replies.content: @Brandon Maloney honestly, it's just the fact that I get to talk about what I love. like I genuinely love the neuroscience & psychology behind productivity. explaining the why behind what we do is just so fascinating to me. so when someone asks me, "but what do I do if I can't stay focused?", I'm able to not only give them the exact actionable steps that work, but also give them the biological reasoning as to why it works. that way, they also know how to manipulate this tactic because they know not just the actions, but the mechanisms behind it.

replies.author: Brandon Maloney
replies.content:
@Aathraey Shrikanth that's awesome bro, that made me smile.
:)👽

comment.author: Lukas Schmidt
comment.content:
Why not both?
I think of motivation as 4 core drives:
Positive motivation
Negative motivation
Extrinsic motivation
Intrinsic motivation
The more we stack on top the more motivated we are to do anything.

comment.author: Germain-Blaise Nkede

But push is what carries you on the days when that feeling isn’t there, because let’s be real, it won’t always be. For me, I’ve learned I can’t rely on just one. Pull gives direction and purpose, push builds discipline and consistency. The people who win are the ones who can operate with both depending on the moment.

original_post.author: Scott Northwolf
original_post.title: What's the soul?
original_post.body:
@Michael Pluszek recently made a post that called my attention (https://www.skool.com/synthesizer/soul-vs-identity?p=21a05ba9) and I gave a brief introductory explanation of the structure of reality based on the Hermetic Tradition Model.
I called it part one of my answer and here it is:
For clarity and simplicities' sake I'll omit some details and break it down into a simplistic model developed by the ancient Hermetics.
Reality is structured in different layers of organization.
1-Source consciousness (the void, the one, what ancient Greeks in the Pythagorean tradition used to call the Monad).
2-The Mental Plain (The Duat).
3-The causal plain (where there's an observer, an object and a subject, you need three elements to have an experience, you need an object, a subject and an option, an experience itself).
4-The Astral Plain (what's called the Quantum Field of Possibilities, where ideas, language, feelings and imagination exist as components of reality we can percieve).
5-Physical reality (the manifestation and integration of all the above components).
To understand identity and the soul we need to understand first this structure because it's foundational to the understanding that flows from this. If we don't take this as the basis the rest will not make sense and to explain why this is so it will take... well, a lot of time, so let's just suspend disbelief and accept this framework as a precodition for the following knowledge to unfold itself.
If you want to get the full explanation just let me know and I'll write the second part of this...
///////////// ///////////// ///////////// ///////////// /////////////
Since some of you, guys, requested for the second part here it goes ( @Lea Newkirk @Victor Floden @Michael Pluszek ):
The soul is an unchanging configuration of pure consciousness, an emanation of the Source at the level of the mental and causal plain linking them both together.
Mental Plane → Soul → Causal Plane → Astral → Physical
The soul serves the function of incarnating into the physical plain, it has two main paths:
It will either descend into density by entangling itself with matter and the lower insticts generating karma or...
It will release karma through 'gnosis' (knowlege of its divine essence) and return to source through a purification process.
This is not as simple as I'm painting it to be.
Souls on their path out of source and back to it won't follow a linear downwards and then upwards trend during a cycle of reincarnations until EVERYTHING returns to source, it will look more like EVERY single possible combination ascending during some reincarnations, going 'downwards' during others and ascending again and like that on many different ways throughout all the possible reincarnations that can occur during a full cycle.
This cycles can be viewed as a cosmic breath of sorts.
I'd love to answer any questions that you guys might come up in the comments and clarify everything in case I haven't explained it with enough clarity for you.

comment.author: Kai Cerar
comment.content: Wow!! This is soooo deep man!!!

replies.author: Scott Northwolf
replies.content: @Kai Cerar yeah, I know... got some requests to talk about it... hope you got something useful out of it. I rather give actionable steps on how to use all of this for practical purposes but maybe this post will spark that kind of questions and I'd be glad to answer them.

replies.author: Kai Cerar
replies.content: @Scott Northwolf YES!! Actionable steps are the best man!

replies.author: Scott Northwolf
replies.content:
@Kai Cerar one of the most powerful things this can do for you is first of all shifting the mindset from the victim's perspective to the main character's perspective.
How would your life change if you knew that EVRYTHING that happens in your life is not happening TO you but FOR you and that you have chosen it all from your higher perspective in order for you to learn a lesson and grow through awareness.
Wouldn't it lift a huge weight from your shoulders to start 'playing' at life instead of struggling though it?
Detatchment from outcome creates a kind of confident and playful relaxation that allows us to face challenges with an unexorable and relentless mental fortitude...
This allows us to actually dream big and set huge goals but also reverse engineer them into actionable steps that we can undertake daily in order to acheive such dreams.
If you want you can share what's you ultimate goal for your life and we can map it into your current day to day to see how you are making progress consistently towards such goal.

replies.author: Kai Cerar
replies.content: @Scott Northwolf bro this is beautiful thanks!!

replies.author: Kai Cerar
replies.content: @Scott Northwolf my ultimate life goal honestly changes a lot day by day. I’m just enjoying the process, but I do think that a vision is very important.

replies.author: Scott Northwolf
replies.content:
@Kai Cerar maybe taking one full day to do a full-blown dopamine detox and journal very deeply about what you want out of this life will help.
I do an exercise with my clients for goal setting which is VERY powerful.
We humans are like ships, we set sail and go into the ocean, a lot of people end up stranded on a desert island wondering why they didn't become millionaires or something like that (let's say becoming a millionaire would be in this metaphor arriving at Bristol, England and let's say you set sail from Boston).
Well... did you ever set a goal to actually get there?
Imagine a ship going into the ocean without compass, without a map or a port of destination...
Hard to see meaning on the voyage if you don't really know where you are going.
Enjoying the process is a very important part of it but having a powerful motivation is a game changer.

replies.author: Scott Northwolf
replies.content:
@Jack Shiller yeah, when I came across it for the first time I thought the same and I saw some traditions differing about it but i opted for the model of the Ancient Greeks (Pythagorians, Platonists, Neo-Platonists) and the Hermetics (as well as the Mediaval Alchemists, but those guys went crazy overcomplicating the whole thing)...
They explain that the structure is the following:
1-Monad (source, void).
2-Duat (Mental plain).
3-Triad (Causal plain: it would sound logical that if you have two things you can see cause an effect but in reality is not possible because if there's not a third 'thing', an observer then there's no choice to be made, there's no distinction between you and the other thing because there's no awareness of you as such if there's no separation, if there's no YOU, let's say, and also an awareness or consiousness component within you that is observing yourself and the other 'thing' with which you are interacting then there's no causality, I hope I explained myself correctly there).
4-Tetrad (Astral plain, the inifinte realm of possibilities, the quantum field of possibilities, ideas, concepts, emotions, imaginations, all that we can percieve through the third eye).
5-Pentad (Physical plain, 'material' reality, what Buddhists call Maya, illusion, the realm we percieve through our senses).
PS: That's the Tree of Life representing the Ten Sefirot from the Jewish Kabbalah. The Jewish Kabbalah is an esoteric tradition taken from the ancient Egyptian Prists and Farohs by Moses and revealed trough him to the Jewish people. It's good stuff. Most pathways lead to the top of the mountain. From the top the view is always the same.

replies.author: Jack Shiller
replies.content: @Scott Northwolf I'm with you on that order, it's maybe a "terminology" difference for the Duat. But that basically sums up the formless --> form there

Can't describe this feeling in words!
Literally made my first $ (300$) of my life and
I can't describe this feeling, literally, no amount of drugs, alcohol, sex, women, nothing has made me this happier than my first hard earned money.

@Andrew Kirby inspired me

Years ago and had a dream of how this feeling might be like and finally experienced it today!
Thanks Kirby ❤Thanks @Aidan LaBreche ❤️
Side note: I know the video looks a bit cringe 🌞
but fk it.
I got the W ⭐❤️⛰️

comment.content: brooooooo, so amazing to see! Really glad for you, man! Keep up the good work. Also… please expand… how did it happen? What’s the story, what’s the offer? How was the sales’ call and how did you arrived at that? I’d love to know it ALL!!!

replies.author: Piyush Mahankal
replies.content:
@Scott Northwolf Oh, I'd love to expand the story!
So basically, I just cold dmed many people in the quit porn group regarding my free offer:
I found a group and saw the active people, messaged them that:
Hey, I saw you in this group and wanted to connect with you.
I've just started a 7 day free challenge in my community for helping people quit porn and recover from ED (Erectile Dysfunction) this was my free offer/ lead magnet.
After that, I got him in my group one month ago, and we booked our first call, I took his situation understood it completely and then understood his problems, after the call.
It was pretty late, 1 AM for me...
i tried to sleep but I wasn't able to, so I just decided why not just implement the free work for him.
I took the call recording and just sunthesized it, filled the worksheet and then decided to create a 30 day 4 week roadmap for him to recover from ED.
I created the roadmap, till 3 AM it was done!
I was so happy after the execution of work and then later I texted him that I had created a roadmap and would you like to jump on a call, he said he didn't have time that week so I just thought why not create a loom and just explain him how the roadmap works!
I gave him the action steps for week 1 and took daily accountability, until next week!
He was happy with the free work I did and till the next call 7 days were already over!
So I talked to him for an hour and later in the end of the call, I had done the implementation of one step of the multi step process!
It was like I've given you free bread. Would you like some peanut butter?
(Peanut butter was something that I had to charge for)
So I did! Eventually. I told my price and it was 2000$ but he sadly that amount was too much for him at that moment, so I said how about 300$ !
Then boom. Closed!
🔥That was my story! And the next day, the peanut butter was soo good that he told me, he didn't expect this is ws to be that great!
I'm happy that he's happy with the work.

original_post.author: Scott Northwolf
original_post.title: You vote with your behaviour, not your words...
original_post.body:
What are you saying to people you'll do but you are not actually doing and procrastinating on it?
How many times have you started with something just to drop it as soon as it gets hard?
When was the last time you stuck to something for MORE than an entire YEAR and actually did the WORK consistently?
Delusion backed up just by words it's just delusion but delusion backed up by relenstless action... that's called FAITH, brother! Remember: Duty, Honor and Pride!

comment.author: Lea Newkirk
comment.content: Consistency is the highest form of self-respect. It’s one thing to manifest with words, but staying regulated and showing up for the work for over a year, especially when the newness wears off, is where the real transformation happens. This post is truly golden!

replies.author: Scott Northwolf

replies.author: Lea Newkirk
replies.content: @Scott Northwolf

replies.author: Lea Newkirk

replies.author: Lea Newkirk

replies.content: @Scott Northwolf I do not have enough words in my word box to explain how amazing this technique is, especially the part about manifesting through actions. The world needs your light, keep shining!

replies.content: @Kenneth Khamphiphone I'm very careful with what I say, because I have managed to make my word so valuable that IT IS LAW. If I say something will happen... it always happens. Imagine developing such power... trust me when I tell you it's one of the most fulfilling things you can experience as a man.

original_post.author: Scott Northwolf
original_post.title: Don't digress. Be simple...
original_post.body:
Recently I was on a coaching call with one of my clients and at a certain point I was going rounds and rounds trying to break one of his limiting beliefs and he just said to me "Brother, be simple. Get to the point."
I could tell how tired he was of me just repeating myself every time we'd disagree and I realized then...
"Wait a minute! Why is this not working? Why is this taking so long? Why do I feel like I'm speaking for ages and getting nowhere?"
Then it struck me even harder when I was on two separate calls with other coaches while we were talking about business.
They'd digress like crazy and it'd feel like pure ADHD every step of the conversation. Then I realized they were a mirror to my own behaviour and I could tell how tiring it was the whole deal.
Since then I have decided to try to keep it simple.
Condensed the biggest amount of high leverage knowledge in the shortest sentences possible and focus on actionable steps way more than on overexplaining super complex concepts just to satisfy my own ego and show just how smart I am (I am, and also the most humble man in the universe in case you haven't been able to tell by now).
Being comfortable speaking less and even remaining in silence it's an art and it brings you more power, not less.
Striking the balance is essential.
Speak too much and you'll come across weak and as if you have something to proof.
We all have something to proof but we must start by proving that to ourselves so it comes off naturally and it's projected from a place of abundance, groundness and relaxation instead of from a place of lack and fear.
Delusion backed up just by words it's just delusion but delusion backed up by relentless action... that's called FAITH, brother! Remeber: Duty, Honor and Pride!!!

original_post.author: Harry Montford
original_post.title: What supplements have boosted your productivity?
original_post.body:
I've seen some supplements completely change people's productivity...
The main one I take is creatine daily. A lot of people take this for weight lifting but it also has GREAT impacts on your brain.
it replenishes ATP levels in our brain which is our main energy source.
This obviously improves your cognitive function but mainly during times you feel tired and low in energy.
So I wanted to ask, do you take any supplements to enhance your productivity?
If you have, did you notice any improvements in your productivity?
Looking forward to seeing answers from fellow synthesizers!

comment.author: Sandra Torres
comment.content:
Before asking “what supplement boosts productivity?”, I think the real question is: what is draining it?
In my experience, productivity improves far more when you remove what depletes your system than when you keep adding things.
A few fundamentals changed everything for me:
1. Nervous system first: If your autonomic nervous system is constantly in stress mode, your brain will always feel tired.
Deep sleep, daily movement, sunlight, breathing and real rest regulate the system.
2. Energy leaks: Chronic stress, poor sleep, overworking, constant stimulation, ultra-processed food… all of that quietly drains cognitive energy.
Sometimes the most powerful step is simply stop, observe and remove what doesn't serve your system.
3. Foundations before optimization: Whole real food, hydration, consistent sleep schedule, daily exposure to nature and Sun and physical training will do more for productivity than most stacks.
Once that base is solid, supplements can support the system.
The ones I personally find useful are simple:
• Creatine – cellular energy (brain included)
• Magnesium – nervous system and sleep quality
• Omega-3 – brain and inflammation balance
• Vitamin D – especially during winter
But for me supplements are supporting actors, not the driver.
Most productivity problems are not solved by adding something new.
They are solved by pausing, removing what drains us, creating space, rebalancing our nervous system and rebuilding our lifestyle in a way that actually supports our biology.
Only then the question becomes meaningful: Is my life structured in a way that allows my brain and body to function at their natural best? Because when that’s the case, productivity stops feeling forced, it becomes a natural consequence.

original_post.author: Piotr Rzepecki
original_post.title: Something I’ve always liked about football (soccer)
original_post.body:
When you’re on the field, everyone has a different role.
Someone defends. Someone creates chances. Someone scores.
But the game only works when people play together.
It reminded me a lot of communities.
A good community isn’t just one person posting.
It’s people sharing ideas, helping each other, and moving things forward together.
Just like in football, the real value appears when everyone is involved ⚽

original_post.author: Lea Newkirk
original_post.title: Everyday is the best day ever! 🥹
original_post.body:
A while ago, I learnt the concept of confirmation bias. Basically, the human tendency to only pay attention to information that proves what we already believe, while ignoring anything that doesn't. The way I act on this is, waking up, deciding it is already the best day ever and my job is to hunt for all the reasons why (at least 3), within minutes I already have so many reasons and I have not had a bad day since 😂 not because bad things don't happen (that's unrealistic) but because regardless, whatever I focus on will confirm what I already believe.
I am very curious, if anyone else does something similar, please share! I would love to hear about your experience and what usually confirms that the day will be an amazing one?

original_post.author: Krista Melanson
original_post.title: An observation from someone a little further down the life arc
original_post.body:
One thing I’ve really enjoyed about spending time in Synthesizer is the energy in the room.
There are a lot of builders here — people thinking deeply about offers, systems, momentum, and the long game of building something meaningful.
I’ve learned a lot just by listening to the conversations.
At the same time, I sometimes realize I’m observing the room from a slightly different vantage point.
Most of the people here are younger entrepreneurs building businesses.
Meanwhile, I’m a little further down the life arc, and my work focuses on helping people build strong, supportive relationships.
So I often find myself quietly watching the conversations about growth, focus, and grinding forward.
From where I sit, something eventually becomes very clear for most people:
At some point, the thing that either supports or destabilizes everything you’re building isn’t strategy.
It’s your relationships.
Timing plays a big role in when people realize that.
So I’m curious:
Have you ever noticed that the people who benefit most from what you do often haven’t reached the moment in their life where they can see it yet?
