comment.author: Simon Schörkhuber
comment.content:
You got a great point there! What I found was that between the first time hearing something and realizing what's behind that and how it can be put to good use, some time has to go by. Sometimes it's a few days and sometimes it may take months or even years.
I also believe that there are some things you only realize later down the line - either with age or experience.
Some of them probably are unteachable lessons - lessons you HAVE to find out yourself. You can hear them over and over again. Study them. Read about them. But you'll only learn them by going all the way.

@Simon Schörkhuber I think that's true. I mean, I think of my kids and the things that I tell them over and over again, and then one day they come to me and say, "Did you know...?" and I'm like, "ummm...Yes."
Have you ever heard the song "Cats In The Cradle"? It's all about that. A father telling his son not to be like him and work too much and miss out on his family, and then one day he realizes his son becomes just like him.

In that respect, sometimes people wait until it's too late, but there are people who prioritize their relationships right from the get-go. I'm that person, and I know I take it to the extreme, but for me, without my relationships, there's no point in working on a business.
That's why I'm trying to promote the idea of staying connected, and suggesting it in small moments rather than saying you need to take a week off and go on vacation, though I do also advocate for that. Ha ha!
Where would you say you are with your work-life balance right now?

original_post.author: Jack Shiller
original_post.title: do you let your fav posts disappear? 🔥
original_post.body:
Sometimes I feel scared of making a banger post to a community if I know that it could fade away into the feed and get forgotten 👶
Yet in many ways, I kinda like that.
On Skool, and especially in Synthesizer, posting to the community feels like it’s based off of the unique energy of the day.
It’s less about how polished the writing is, and more about how attuned it is to the community atmosphere at any given moment.
I try not to be excessive with my moderator powers here in Synthesizer lmao but..
Thought leadership posts fail, honest call-to-action questions are appreciated, and posts that spark conversation work best.
These guidelines help us focus on what’s in front of us TODAY and NOW. Not on what’s in the future.
But it makes me wonder what we can do with those posts that we're proud of, once they've been swept away into the feed.
Asking u guys...
💭 Do you repurpose any of your Skool posts for other content?

comment.author: Benedek Santa
comment.content:
@Jack Shiller Yeah, so I've actually been on Skool at this point for four years and honestly this is a pattern that to be honest annoys me a little bit.
Synthesizer and most groups in the past would be much more focused on just everyone posting bangers and sharing unique takes and things like that, and we created this mastermind effect. But I think in the last one or two years people discovered a new meta, and unpopular opinion, but I think actually this new meta sucks.
Because people realized that if they just start asking basic questions from people, that's a very good way to engagement farm.
I saw someone the other day ask what's your favorite color. I'm over here spending hours making posts and others make a post about what's your favorite color. Which I don't have a problem with. I just have a problem with it on the deeper psychological side of that thing. Because everyone wants to be heard. So posts that make people feel like they are heard and put the spotlight from yourself into other people perform extremely well.
The issue with these posts is they rarely actually increase the value of a community. And because people just keep having conversations there, sometimes I've also seen from other people genuinely solid posts, but they get just low engagement because the best people can say is "oh nice, good work." But when you ask people what's their favorite color, they jump on that thing instantly because that's a very easy way to engage.
(It has a low cognitive load to be engaged with.)
What I believe in Synthesizer we should actually have a rule around this, about not asking questions that are just about engagement farming.
But that's just my unique take and as I said I've been in this group for four years I think. So I've seen a lot of patterns happen, but this is one that's not really my favorite I think. And I'm actually really trying to go back to the point in this group where the group was actually more like a mastermind and all of us were posting value and the best post was pinned not because it was a win or not because it was a question, but because someone actually just published a banger.
What you think?

comment.author: William Porter
comment.content:
@Paul Neri Most new offers start from the founder seeing a gap. (A problem big enough that someone would pay for that everyone misses or does not do good enough)
The truth is that most problems are solved for already. Its more about what you choose to focus on and how you consistently solve them. Some pick small problems and others pick issues that dont really exist in the market.
So I would start from your personal experience. What have you wished people did the right way?
Then ask youself. Is this a big enough problem? Someone should already be doing this. How are they solving for this.
Remember. Competition is a signal of profit in a market. Even Uber has competitors and they solved for a new problem of a modern era that taxis already solved.
Start with the problem and narrow down on this. Then the offer will be easy to put together.

original_post.author: Christy Sanderson
original_post.title: When I Stopped Reposting and Started Proving
original_post.body:
There was a time when I could articulate powerful ideas.
Clarity. Discipline. Identity. Structure. Consistency.
I could write about them. Teach them. Quote them.
But there was a quiet gap between what I said and what I had actually tested under pressure.
I remember one particular week — overwhelmed, financially stretched, emotionally tired. I had been speaking about consistency and structure publicly… but privately, my own systems were fragile.
That’s when it hit me.
It’s easy to repost wisdom when life is calm.
It’s different to live it when life pushes back.
My pain point wasn’t hypocrisy.
It was realization.
I had absorbed powerful ideas from books, podcasts, communities — and I believed them. But belief is not embodiment.
Pressure reveals what you’ve actually integrated.
So I made a decision.
I stopped sharing ideas I hadn’t personally run through fire.
I stopped echoing what sounded impressive.
I started asking:
Does this hold up when I’m tired?
Does this work when money is tight?
Does this help when doubt creeps in?
Does this produce results consistently?
And something changed.
My voice got quieter — but stronger.
My content got simpler — but sharper.
Because now, when I say something works, it’s not because it sounds right.
It’s because I’ve watched it survive stress.
There’s a difference between information and proof.
Reposting spreads ideas.
Pressure-testing builds authority.
And authority built through lived experience can’t be shaken by trends.
What idea have you personally pressure-tested that most people only repost?

Congrats on getting that realization. It takes a lot to reflect and think about it that way - but at some point that's part of the Synthesizer journey I guess.

replies.author: Christy Sanderson
replies.content:
@Michael Pluszek Thank you so much for sharing this and for being so open about your journey. Your post truly shows the power of lived experience. I love how you’ve broken down your life lessons into concrete experiences, from managing debt and building businesses to navigating relationships and personal growth. It’s a reminder that the most valuable insights don’t always come from books or theory they come from walking through life fully, facing challenges, and learning from them.
I really resonate with your approach of sharing openly as it comes to you, without relying on a rigid plan or a “content schedule.” There’s authenticity and rawness in that which I believe draws people in and creates real impact. It’s inspiring to see someone consistently turn their life experiences both the highs and the difficult phases into lessons that can help others avoid some of the same pain, or at least see the path forward more clearly.
I also appreciate how you highlight that it’s not just about success, but also about the dark phases, the setbacks, and the moments where trust and patience were key. That’s what makes your teaching relatable and grounded in reality. It’s proof that growth comes not only from what works, but also from how we respond when things don’t.
I’d love to hear more about how you decide which lessons to share first. With such a rich tapestry of experiences from personal, business, to leadership how do you choose what to surface next so it has the most impact? Also, what has surprised you most about the way people receive and apply the lessons you share?
Your openness is a gift to this community, and I can’t wait to see what other wisdom you choose to put out next.

original_post.author: Avi Molina
original_post.title: Are we chasing an ilussion of freedom or living in true freedom?
original_post.body:
Hey Synthesizers, I wanted to create this post to be super honest, to open my heart, to share very real human emotions I deal with as a leader and because I know that life as an entrepreneur is intense! Some days, you are stepping into your fullest potential and some other days you are feeling under the weather...
Those days, in which I am not in the best mentality, could feel like the easiest thing to do is just quit or try something new, just let go of everything... again.
I mean; How many times has this cycle shown up in your reality?
Let's speak raw and real, unfiltered truth. Not every stage of business is going to be roses and rainbows. Not all calls are going to feel inspiring. Not all launches will have the results you calculated. And that doesn't mean that you are not progressing or that it isn't working.
I have been there so many times! Throwing everything away and starting again, and again and again, doubting if this is for me at all... feeling like s&*t honestly 😂 but with a voice that continued saying; you are in the right path, continue, continue walking.
You see, most people will quit in THAT stage (you know what I am talking about) or have major breakdowns. But the sooner you realize that this is a game and that life has its ebbs and flows and that you are going to be okay in them. The sooner you get grounded in your physical reality and realize that it is okay to be where you are at, then you'll find true peace in your journey, and that's the exact place where you'll find your inner leader.
That leader that is not scared to fail, because they know that they can just try another route. That leader that has conviction about a bigger vision that is not only about themselves. That leader that is able to hold others that are stepping into something new...
In here, I find a community of leaders. And I would like to know about you, but, the raw and real you, the leader who can hold truth and is not scared of speaking auhtentically. The brave one who has walked the talk.
What are the practices/philosophies that have helped you in your journey? How do you anchor yourself in the most difficult times? How do you keep doing what you need to do even when you feel like it's not going to work out the way you want?
Would love to connect with other humans!!!! Not people pretending that everything is perfect but true leaders

original_post.author: Brandon Smith
original_post.title: The Three C's of time-wasting (which one do you fall into?)
original_post.body:
Most people worry that they don't have enough time in their day.
But after years of working in the military, becoming an educational creator, and building my own business, I found that almost all time-waste falls into what I call "The 3 C's"
1. Consuming (The Dopamine Trap)
When it comes to the "7 C's" of Synthesizer, the first one is about "Consume".
And for good reason.
We become the collection of things that we consume.
And if we're consuming garbage, that not only wastes time, it wastes your own potential to grow as a human.
We need to be extremely aware of what we decide to enter our minds.
The solution for any new Synthesizers out there: you will find extreme value "consuming" the first phase of the roadmap: Orient. It can save you literal years of your life if you apply its teachings.
The most important concept I learned from Phase 1 is this:
Just-in-case consumption, and Just-in-time consumption.
Just-in-case consumption means you try to store as much information in your mind as possible before getting started. You listen to business podcasts in the background, all of the Hormozi content, and "absorb knowledge."
But this creates more overwhelm than anything.
Just-in-time consumption, however, is when you only consume WHEN you need it the most. This leads to fast breakthroughs, more efficiency, and less overwhelm.
2. Complaining (The Victim Trap)
I don't think I've ever heard a time where complaining has ever helped someone's situation in life (I'd like to be proven wrong at this lol)
But seriously. People like to complain about everything, even the most minor inconveniences.
When people choose to complain, they make themselves a victim to their own circumstances. They blame everything in the world for where they're at, rather than taking responsibility.
Yes, there are people with legitimate problems in life. And life is unfair.
But the people who refused to let their life circumstance define them and take responsibility have often seen great success.
Instead of focusing on why the situation sucks, ask yourself "what is the one thing I can control that can help me overcome this?"
3. Contemplating (The Decision Fatigue Trap)
We have to make thousands of little decisions each day. And as Synthesizers, we purposely set ourselves up to make even more decisions like solving problems.
The "problem" with that is it can affect your ability to commit to decisions.
You contemplate whether to send that post or send that DM.
You contemplate whether you want to go to the gym or not.
You contemplate whether you're gonna have Eggs or Cereal for breakfast.
Contemplating on all these decisions is what's truly eating away your time.
The fix: Make your life simple.
Plan your day in advance
Set up your environment to make it easy to get things done
Commit to one decision at a time
We can always worry about "what tasks have more leverage than the other", but if you're just getting started, taking any meaningful action and sticking with it is infinitely more leverage than inaction.
There's also a post created by Andrew Kirby that solves this problem well, and it's called the GPS Framework: Goal, Problem, Solution
For example:
Goal: What am I trying to achieve? (e.g., First $10k month).
Problem: What is the actual constraint that if solved will make the goal inevitable? (e.g., Not enough people know I exist).
Solution: Spend the entire week doing nothing else but solving the problem (e.g., The Rule of 300, 100 minutes of content, comments, and DM's).
Everything else is just noise.
When you cut out the 3 Cs, you don’t just find more time—you find your purpose again.
Which of these 3 "Cs" do you struggle with the most right now—Consuming, Complaining, or Contemplating?

original_post.author: Melanie Naumann
original_post.title: Finally paid off my credit card debt 🥳
original_post.body:
I have been working on my online business for 7 years now.
7 years of trying to make it work.
7 years of not making any income.
7 damn years of investing into programs to find a reliable system that works.
(And believe me, I’ve been through a lot, Blogs, Websites, Courses, Funnels, Challenges, Ads, Books, Social Media strategies…)
7 years only spending money with very low returns.
And I got burned pretty bad with the last coach I had.
So for the last 2 years, I did not invest into any program any more.
The credit card debt I had from the previous investment was still growing.
And then, I found Synthesizer.
And I remained skeptical.
Stayed on the sidelines and watched.
And I saw the success stories.
I talked with those members.
And I saw more success stories.
And more.
And at one point, there was no question anymore:
“If they can, so can I.”
I believed this was the roadmap, the direction that I so desperately needed.
I joined Synthesizer Scaling in January, one of the last open spots.
I did not have the money.
I had to go into more credit card debt.
And I was f* scared.
But the path that I was on, was obviously not working.
I had to make a change.
And I believed I could make that change with @Aidan LaBreche and @Andrew Kirby 's help
So, I did.
I joined Synthesizer Scaling in January this year.
Two months later, yesterday, I paid off my credit card debt. 🥳
I feel so relieved. Finally free.
Finally, I can look towards the dream that I have, and do the roadtrip with my daughter that I always wanted to. I can now save for that. And know how to make the money for it.
And I know, this is only the beginning. 🌞
So, if you struggle with not knowing if taking the next step is the right thing to do, jump on a call with them. Who knows, it can be the start of something you never thought would be possible. 💚

original_post.author: Thomas El Nadir
original_post.title: The Genius behind the Synthesizer Group and Roadmap
original_post.body:
I’m kindly but surely making my way through the Roadmap, and I’m amazed by how fluid the whole process feels.
I started with Phase 1, Orient, and it showed me how I had always identified with the Synthesizer identity without realizing it. At the same time, the group gave me a direct opportunity to connect with like-minded people.
By following the action steps, I reinforced that sense of belonging through commenting and connecting. Naturally, I reached Level 2, where I could post and share my learnings, anchoring that feeling even deeper. We learn better by teaching, which perfectly reflects the Synthesizing philosophy to me.
Now for the crazy part:
I moved on to Phase 2, Network, watching one video a day. As I did, things opened up in the group in perfect sync with what I was learning. I could immediately experience each concept in real time.
Everything clicked when just as I reached the Chat video, I also reached Level 5, which allowed me to start chats with community members.
Commenting was the first step, and sparks were naturally created.
Then I watched the Chat video, and those initial comments organically led to DMs with members of the group.
And the very next day after watching the Call video, I had an amazing call with @Scott Northwolf where we exchanged deep thoughts and philosophies about life, manifestation, and creating our dream realities.
Nothing was planned or forced. It unfolded naturally as I followed the roadmap and applied the action steps.
The main realization I want to share is this: the Synthesizer Group is the perfect sandbox to practice everything we learn in the Roadmap. You don’t just consume the information. You integrate it through direct experience, immediately.
And the best part?
You don’t need to strategize or force anything. Simply following the action steps makes everything fall into place.
So if you’re starting or revisiting the roadmap, my suggestion is to take action right after each video, instead of binge-watching them all at once.
Props and gratitude to @Andrew Kirby and @Aidan LaBreche for this process, especially considering it’s all FREE! It’s inspiring to see how radical simplicity, the right information at the right time, and a space to apply it directly create learning that truly sticks and produces real results.
Phase 3, here I am!
Looking forward to connecting with more of you in chats and calls. I’m always happy to exchange thoughts and perspectives about Synthesizing and Life 🔥

original_post.author: Lara Bruyelles
original_post.title: How I went from 0 to 7 paying clients ($4k+) in 3 months (with no audience)
original_post.body:
I have an 8-week program
my 1st client paid $200
2nd 3rd 4th: 500$
5th: 800$
6th 7th: 1000$
I wouldn't be here without this amazing community.
I've been trying to build something online for years, and since I joined Synthesiser, I met amazing people, and something clicked.
You don't have to be an expert or the guru at the top of the mountain to start coaching.
In my experience, all you need is
Enough calls
A clear offer
Conviction in what you sell
The most beautiful part for me has been to give free value through posts, dms and calls.
I learned the concept of "beautiful money" from this amazing community and about the importance of over-giving so that you get happy clients.
Concretely, what did I do?
I figured out what specifically I wanted to help people with, even if I didn't have much experience in it
I set a price low enough that I could feel confident enough to do sales calls
I did NOT do free work
I set a goal of spending at least 1h on Skool every day in communities where my target audience engages
I jumped on enough calls to get a yes
Confidence builds over time, and even after signing my seventh client, I still experience discomfort and doubt through the whole process
One thing that's sure is that I've truly stepped into my purpose, and that I'm helping people deeply, getting paid to change the world
So Synthesizers, what are you waiting for?

original_post.author: Aathraey Shrikanth
original_post.title: Do you multitask?
original_post.body:
When I first heard multi-tasking messed up my productivity, I thought it wasn't much. Maybe like 10-20% max (which is still a lot)?
But then I came across this article... I completely quit it.
"Although switch costs may be relatively small, sometimes just a few tenths of a second per switch, they can add up to large amounts when people switch repeatedly back and forth between tasks. Thus, multitasking may seem efficient on the surface but may actually take more time in the end and involve more error. Meyer has said that even brief mental blocks created by shifting between tasks can cost as much as 40 percent of someone's productive time."
You can lose up to 40% of your focus from just SWITCHING TASKS (switch cost).
And this is explains why even though you might work for hours, you still don't get much done.
So here's my question to you: did you use to multitask? if so, what are you going to do to stop it?

original_post.author: Scott Northwolf
original_post.title: ☠️ Blasphemy at Synthesizer ❌
original_post.body:
Soooooooo...
I know how much we praise the power of networking calls and I know about our roadmap and stuff but...
Lately I've been feeling so excited about my offer (it happens quite often to be fair, especially every time I improve it) and I have done so much networking that I feel like I'm not impacting as deeply people's lives by jumping on a call and sharing ideas and knowledge when they are very likely not to apply them. It feels kind of shallow and unsubstancial sometimes.
And I'm not saying I haven't gotten WONDERFUL connections, friendships and collaborations out of all those networking calls (shout out to @J'son Elizondo, @Jack Shiller, @John Fuchs and so many other wonderful synthesizers I have met here, sorry for not mentioning you all, brothers)...
But I know for a fact how radically I change the lives of the people I directly work with as my clients. I know because some of them are even living HERE in Bali because of it and it is SUCH an amazing thing to witness and be able to be part of.
So what I want to say now and put out there for debate is...
I quite honestly feel the highest leverage thing is to strike a balance between networking calls and sales' calls.
And by balance I don't mean 50/50. I mean a proper paretto distribution, something along the lines of 20% (networking calls) 80% (sales' calls).
For example, I'm doing right now around 10 calls per week (actually more but I want to be pessimistic always with the numbers). Best thing I can do and I'm planning to do is to engage only in 2 networking calls per week and 8 sales' calls or so.
I know how much more high leverage it is...
And the money is nice, and don't get me wrong, I love it, I'm not going to pose here as puritane or anything and try to send some virtue signaling and pretending I'm some sort of charity when I'm not.
But I know also for a FACT [and I CARE very deeply about this (and even out of ego or whatever you SHOULD care about this as well)] that the BIGGEST impact I'll make is not by talking without taking action and just sharing ideas and networking but by actually DOING and the only way I get to DO and COMMIT very deeply to people (and they to me) is by getting them on my program and helping them achieve their desired outcome.
You see my point here or am I sounding crazy to you?
I'd love to read your thoughts on the matter, fellow Synthesizers.
Definetely at the beginning stages networking and research calls should be the bulk of your meetings but I do believe everything works in seasons and behaves in a rythmic manner. But maybe that's just me (but I know I'm right, still feel free to argue... 🤣).

original_post.author: Terrell Jones
original_post.title: Stability Comes Before Scale
original_post.body:
The other day, a coach told me they wanted to go full time in their business.
I understood the urge immediately.
That pull is real.
It also reminded me of one of the most common mistakes I see coaches make.
They go full time too early.
Before you scale, before you turn up ads, before you try to grow faster, you need stability. That means consistent lead flow and predictable revenue. Without that foundation, growth just amplifies pressure.
I learned this the hard way.
Back in 2020, I had a strong feeling my job wasn’t going to last much longer. Nothing had happened yet, but I could feel the shift coming. Instead of forcing a leap, I decided to give my business my full effort while I still had the safety net.
At the time, I wasn’t ready to go full time.
The income wasn’t consistent enough.
So I focused on stabilizing the system.
I made sure leads were coming in regularly.
I made sure revenue didn’t spike one month and disappear the next.
I made sure the business could cover its own expenses without constant stress.
Within less than 10 months, I had multiple five-figure months. That consistency is what allowed me to go full time when the transition actually happened.
If I had jumped earlier without that stability, I might have survived short term, but I wouldn’t have sustained it.
That’s the part that often gets skipped in conversations about growth.
Scaling works best when your systems are already doing their job. Once you know a certain amount of revenue is coming in each month, you can confidently put two or three times more money into ads and let growth happen without panic.
Before you scale, what tells you your coaching business is actually stable?

original_post.author: Auðunn Hartmannsson
original_post.title: The only thing worth falling in love with
original_post.body:
Most people fall in love with the idea of being a creator.
The freedom. The audience. Working online.
That fades when growth is slow.
But if you fall in love with solving problems? And even better... a specific problem? That's different.
Problems don't disappear when motivation drops.
Your audience will always struggle with the same issues.
When you love solving it, feedback becomes useful instead of painful.
You're not asking "do they like my content?"
You're asking "did this help?"
You stop worrying about what to post. You just ask: does this solve their problem?
You stop stressing about your offer. You just refine it until it works better.
That's what I'm striving for. Falling in love with solving problems for my niche.
What about you?

original_post.author: Harry Montford
original_post.title: How do you solve your constraints?
original_post.body:
Everyone struggles with scaling their business at one point.
And how you solve the constraints can determine whether you make no money online.
Or reach $10k per month or even $100k per month in a couple of years...
I solve constraints using something called Socratic Questioning (a problem solving method developed by Socrates).
Essentially you ask yourself why at least 5 times to get to the root cause of a problem. Since what we think is a problem is usually just a symptom.
This can be great for finding productivity constraints but also problems in your business you don't see.
Something I found when doing this is the root cause of constraints is something we don't expect at all. You might think it's your conversion, when it's actually the amount of leads you are getting.
So how do you find constraints? I think this could really help some Synthesizers scale much faster if they knew what to do.
Comment below!

original_post.author: Eileen MacDonell
original_post.title: What would your eulogy say?
original_post.body:
I was just on a coffee call with another Skooler I met at a networking event in another Synthesizer’s community, and I asked him... "Let's say you died yesterday, what would your eulogy say?"
And by the way, I said yesterday so this way I'm not calling in his passing. It didn't happen yesterday so we're good.
He was definitely struck by the question. I mean, who asks that?!
Well, me.
I honestly wanted to know what he envisioned himself doing for the rest of his life.
Who was he?
What impact did he make in his life?
Where did he go?
His answer was pretty common... he said, "I usually plan things out 5 years in advance and I never thought beyond that very much."
That's so common. We're so busy trying to figure out strategy, getting it right, getting seen, living in the tomorrow of our future without really experiencing it. Without really being in intentional action, conscious of how we want to experience our lives.
That's my question for you to contemplate today... if you passed yesterday, what would your eulogy say?
Then, live from that space. Every. Fcking. Day.

original_post.author: Jose Guerra
original_post.title: Feeling behind is heavier than being wrong.
original_post.body:
In a few communities I've been exposed to, hesitation shows up more than participation.
I'm coming to realize that community members are just like high school students.
This mindset has spread across all domains and I see it particularly in paid programs.
People don't ask questions because they feel like they're going to be seen as dumb or that they "should know about this already".
People, don't ask questions because they're afraid of what other people might think, and so they start feeling like they're been left behind and end up classifying the program as "not for them".
I actually, I tested this out yesterday.
I'm taking a workshop about pricing your software and there was this concept that was "tricky", and I saw through their faces that they were confused but nobody raised their hand or decided to ask a question and so I did.
And no joke, three people, back to back to back, started opening the mic and asking questions that were particularly related to their own situation
Is it just me, or are you seeing this issue in your program as well?

original_post.author: Michael Pluszek
original_post.title: You know what to do — but you don’t do it....
original_post.body:
You’ve bought the programs.
Read the books.
Watched the videos.
Done the research.
And still…the results don’t come.
Not because you don’t understand it.
But because you don’t actually do the thing.
I know this place very well.
Years ago, I was $1.5M in debt.
Before that, I had already written my first three books.
Knowledge wasn’t the problem.
Strategy wasn’t the problem.
I wasn’t selling.
Not because I didn’t know how —but because I didn’t dare.
And if I’m honest, deeper than that:
my nervous system was keeping me stuck before I even took the steps.
When there was finally no other way out, things changed fast.
The company grew to ~60 people in four years.
But not because I found some magic strategy.
Strategy mattered — but only a little.
What really changed was this:
I overcame the internal fight.
And even deeper — my nervous system finally settled enough for me to move.
Selling stopped being a threat.
Action stopped feeling dangerous.
That’s something I had never addressed before.
So I’m curious —what do you experience as the real challenge when it comes to selling?

original_post.author: Auðunn Hartmannsson
original_post.title: I moved to another country to chase this
original_post.body:
A year ago, I was working a job that wasn’t taking me where I wanted to go.
Today? I’m unpacking boxes in Denmark. Quit the job. Left everything familiar behind. Going all in on building something of my own.
And here’s the thing.
I’m not hoping this works. I know it will.
Not because I’m lucky or special. But because I spent the last 2 years proving it to myself.
I built the skills. Got the results. Helped real people solve real problems. Collected proof that this isn’t just an idea, it’s a system that works.
Most people wait for permission to pursue their dream.
They wait for…
- More money saved
- More certainty
- More experience
- The “right time”
But here’s what I learned:
You don’t need permission. You need proof.
And you get proof by doing the work before you feel ready.
I didn’t quit my job the day I had the idea. I spent months…
- Testing my offer
- Getting feedback
- Making a large pivot
- Refining my process
- Building confidence through results
By the time I made the move, it wasn’t a leap of faith. It was a calculated decision based on evidence.
So if you’re building something right now while working a job, studying, or doing something else:
You’re not behind. You’re exactly where you need to be.
Use that time to build proof. Stack small wins. Get so good they can’t ignore you.
Then when you’re ready to go all in, it won’t feel reckless. It’ll feel inevitable.
What are you building right now that could become your full-time thing?

original_post.author: Harry Montford
original_post.title: How I halved my work hours (and doubled my income)
original_post.body:
2 years ago I would work 16 hour days on my business...
I stopped going out with friends,
I stopped all my hobbies and would only speak to my family 2 times a day.
But the truth is I didn't want to work long hours. However, if I wanted to sustain my income online I had no other choice.
And like many entrepreneurs I believed if I worked double the hours, I would double my income (which didn't turn out nicely).
As my work hours increased, my income stayed the same. It didn't make any sense.
And as you probably guessed it, eventually I burnt out and quit because it was impossible to sustain.
But when I look back at those "16 hour work days"... They were not focused at all.
It was dead time spent binging YouTube, scrolling on my phone or procrastinating on doing cold outreach.
Only 2 hours of it was actually focused. And that's when I learnt the most important lesson that changed my life and my business:
Work efficiency always destroys work hours.
See I couldn't understand how solo founders were working 4 hours a day and making 10X more than me. But it was simple:
Their work efficiency was 90% and full of precision.
My work efficiency was 20% so I had to compensate for that with super long work days.
And after months of trying to fix my focus and mental performance it finally worked.
Now I work 4-8 hours a day and make 3X more than I did when I was working 16 hours a day.
So if you're struggling to scale your business and you're working super long hours...
I can't emphasise how much fixing my work efficiency changed my life.
1 hour of focused work is worth much more than 4 hours of shallow work.
Thanks a lot for reading. God bless.

original_post.author: Tibor Spoelder
original_post.title: The most HIGH-LEVERAGE thing to do
original_post.body:
In the past year, I’ve hopped on more than 300 calls.
Networking calls
Research calls
Sales calls
And I can honestly say…
It’s the most high-leverage thing I could have done.
I made great connections…
That lead to opportunities (and people joining your community).
I found market resonance…
By collecting data through asking people what they want and struggle with.
I learnt a lot and taught a lot…
Both lead to great mentors, students and clients.
This allowed me to go from $0 to $10k a month in just 7 months.
I see it everywhere:
People in my network
People in scaling
My Clients
The ones who book the most amount of calls are the ones who make the most amount of money.
So my advice?
Hop on calls with literally everyone.
People at the same level
People ahead of you
People behind you
Don’t focus on what you’ll get out of it.
(because it takes time for it to compound)
Focus on doing 30 calls in the next month.
You’ll thank yourself later.

original_post.author: Jack Shiller
original_post.title: how I took action (scared and terrified)
original_post.body:
When I began networking in Synthesizer a while back, it was my first time throwing myself into the horrifying world of "online calls."
I kept thinking: If I just wait until I have a bigger audience.. more proof.. more credibility.. then I’ll finally be able to add value on calls.
But I knew what the path looked like for me if I kept waiting: deteriorating in comfort as my dream life would become more and more of a dream.
So I did it anyways, and I did it scared.
Not only did this lead to making incredible friends... but I eventually found mentorship, started my community, and actually monetized for the first time in years. (thank u @Sam Rathling @Tibor Spoelder @Mirtha Pena @Scott Northwolf and many others)
Fast forward 5 months and when I heard they needed a speaker for a Skool meetup in New York City.
My instant response was, "man, that sounds pretty terrifying and I'd definitely feel anxious speaking there. But fk it, I'm gonna do it. I'm gonna speak at the event."
And you know what, I did feel nervous, awkward, and embarrassed.
And I wasn't all that perfect. @Michael Rizk sent me a short video of the event afterwards and I cringed so hard at this clip of me speaking 😭🤣
But the internal confidence I've felt in my ability to connect with others since then has been immeasurable. It's insane.
So friends, you can plan all you want and theorize all the outcomes ahead of time.
But I encourage you to step out of your analytical mind, follow your heart, and learn through the actual tangible data that you get when you iterate.
Instead of overthinking your offer for another day:
👉 book a research call
👉 have the conversation
👉 get real feedback
💭What’s one area where you'd reallyyyy want to get some feedback right now?

original_post.author: Scott Northwolf
original_post.title: Health🧑‍⚕️, Wealth💰 or Love❤️?
original_post.body:
Community has changed my life and I couldn't be more grateful for the amazing friends and connections I have made here on Synthezier, Skoolers, Adonis' Gang and my own community Self-Improvement Nation, as well as with the IRL community Let's Be Real here in Bali.
I have found my family here, and people who really support me and are there for me no matter what and that's truly such a precious gift difficult to even put into words.
The experiences, the moments we have shared (sweet and bitter) they were all truly beautiful and I shall treasure them forever.
I have discovered that by opening up and sharing your story, your struggles and who you truly are, having the courage to be vulnerable (not by complaining but by actually owning your feelings and understanding them and expressing them without judgment, taking absolute personal responsability for them) is how you find the people who ACTUALLY appreciate you for WHO YOU ARE and not for the image you try to project to the world.
Having the courage to actually speak your mind and ask for what you truly want without fear of rejection and without rejecting yourself before anybody has the chance to... that kind of courage is precious and will bring back enthusiasm about life if you have ever lost it along the way.
So I want to open up this space here with this post for us to connect at this deep level and talk and my question is...
In which of these categories of life are you currently struggling?
1-Health? 🧑‍⚕️
2-Wealth? 💰
or
3-Love?❤️
I'd kindly request fromyou guys to not give unsolicited advice because such a thing sometimes can do more harm than good and to just be here to support and listen and help only if someone actually asks for it.
This is a space to start deep conversations, connect and support each other's on our individual journeys.
I'd encourage you to pick the person you resonate the most with from their comments and ask them to have a "non-networking" call with you. Not to advance any agendas but to actually connect with them and have some genuine curiosity about their lives.
So I hope that you commit yourself to conduct that call TODAY.
Not tomorrow, not the day after... but TODAY.
Because tomorrow it might be too late and you might forget and miss out on meeting perhaps someone that will change your life forever.
Infinite gratitude to @Jack Shiller, @John Fuchs and @Kai Cerar for our wonderful conversations and all the great ideas and moments we have shared. Looking forward to what's next and I hope to see you guys here in Bali soon.

original_post.author: Ayaan Ashfaq
original_post.title: What change in belief gave you the biggest impact?
original_post.body:
I’ve been thinking about this lately. How you view reality depends on your beliefs. It’s such an insane concept if you think about it. Sounds very abstract…
Until you understand one thing, confirmation bias.
Let’s say you THINK you’re bad at crafting offers or you THINK you can’t offer any value to anyone, you will LITERALLY do the actions that confirm that belief.
You will hesitate and stutter.
You will underprice.
You will knowingly self-sabotage.
Terrifying, I know.
Don’t worry tho, there’s good news in this post 😅. This works in the positive direction too.
Change the belief using systems, reps and proof, and confirmation bias will FORCE you to take actions to solidify that belief. This is what most people refer to as “manifesting”.
We’ve all definitely broken some self-limiting beliefs after stepping into business and seeing what’s actually possible…
So what’s one self-limiting belief you broke or a new belief that you formed, that gave you the most progress?

original_post.author: Mirtha Pena
original_post.title: Setbacks & Self Worth...
original_post.body:
This is a very different post then I normally leave you with, but I think sharing my experience can help some amazing entrepreneurs. Lately I’ve been sitting with setback in a very real way, not the "content-hype- making way" ...the lived one.
I’m preparing a workshop around this, and I keep noticing something in the entrepreneurial space that I don’t fully agree with anymore: the idea that if we just “fix our mindset,” everything will fall into place.
If that were true, none of us would struggle.
What we’re dealing with isn’t a mindset problem... It’s outdated software, and outdated doesn’t mean we can just think it away... You can’t think your way out of natural body responses... You change them with reps, over time... Just like the body, the brain doesn’t change in one session.
Right now I’m resting more than I planned to... A lot fell through the cracks. Networking. Content. A workshop launch that now has to move...bunch of commitments, paused. And old thoughts showed up fast: this isn’t enough, I was gaining momentum, now this.
But I know the cost of building success from survival mode... I’ve confused discipline with that "drive" before, when I was actually running on fumes.
So I practice anchoring my self-worth somewhere else:my values, my impact, the quality of my life
Not my output or my pace any more....Sometimes what we call “failure” is just the body tapping the brakes because ambition got ahead of capacity...the capacity of our system, and that's ok, we can adjust capacity.
So, I’m letting things move slower right now. Letting the agenda float... Trusting momentum returns.
If you’re questioning your worth during a setback, pause... Name your values. Name the impact you’ve already made... Look at the life you’re actually living.
We have seasons. This is normal. And the goal was never the destination anyway.
The destination is death.The work is staying with yourself for the whole ride.
Synthesizers, how do you deal with setbacks?

original_post.title: Make 🚢loads of 💰🤑💸 - The Unofficial 100 Challenge
original_post.body:
Hey Synthesizers 👋
I’ve been given permission by @Andrew Kirby to run an unofficial version of The 100 inside Synthesizers, modeled as closely as possible to the original.
This is not a course. This is a 90-day money-making container built around:
A premium annual offer
A free group funnel

Some awesome Synthesizers are already in: @Judy Lee @Arthur Maddocks-Brown @Lucas Alexander @Roshan Nair @Justin Yip @John Fuchs @Ayaan Ashfaq @Chanie Twersky @Zakia Ahm
You’re invited to join us for free — but read carefully to see if this is right for you.
-----
🔥 Who This Is For
This challenge is for Synthesizers who:
Want to scale their Skool fast
Are ready to take massive action every week
Can handle public accountability
Want a simple plan they will actually implement
Are willing to build a premium annual offer
Are committed to showing up consistently for 90 days
Want to build something meaningful, profitable, and repeatable
If you want momentum, clarity, structure, and a real shot at increasing your revenue in 90 days — this is for you.
⛔ Who This Is Not For
This challenge is not for Synthesizers who:
Want to “learn,” but not execute
Cannot commit to the Rule of 300 (5 hours/day, 5 days/week)
Are unwilling to make their numbers public
Want comfort more than results
Don’t have time to prepare before January 1st
Want to guess instead of following a proven model
Are hoping for shortcuts or hacks
If you’re not ready to take massive action, this challenge is not for you.
-----
🗓 Key Dates & Structure
Pre-Challenge Support Calls (Prep Phase)
Fridays @ 1 pm EST — starting December 12th through December 26th, 2025
These calls will help you:
Validate your offer (Watch Kirby's Offer Training)
Set up your funnel (Watch Kirby's Free Group Funnel Training)

Get every piece in place so you do NOT start January 1st half-built
🪟 The 90-Day Challenge Window
During Challenge Support Calls (Execution Phase)
Fridays @ 1 pm EST — starting January 2 through March 27th, 2026
Commencement on Friday, April 3rd @ 1 pm
We will track:
Q1 2026 revenue (Jan–Mar)
MINUS
Q4 2025 revenue (Oct–Dec)
This gives a clean measurement of actual growth — just like the original 100 did.
Each call includes:
Scoreboard review
Bottleneck diagnosis
Simple marching orders for the week
Wisdom Spotlight — 1 high performer sharing what moved the needle
-----
🔧 How We’ll Run This
The entire challenge follows the same simple system designed by Kirby + Hormozi:
🚗 Traffic → 🤗 Free Group → 📞 Discovery Calls → 💸 Premium Annual Offer
There is no complexity.
There is no new framework.
There is no reinventing the wheel.
Just execution.
💸 1. Premium Annual Offer
Before Jan 1, you will:
Define who you help
Define the transformation you deliver
Set your annual price ($1k–$5k)
We’ll use Kirby's offer framework (Offer Worksheet + Offer Training) to make this clear and attractive.
🤗 2. Free Group Funnel
Before Jan 1, you will:

Test the entire path: visitor → group → call booked
The goal is not just to have a free group. The goal is to have a free group that quietly books calls every week.
📞 3. Discovery Calls
During the challenge, you will:

You don’t need to be “good at sales.” You only need to care about helping people and providing value.
🚗 4. Traffic (Rule of 300)
You will run:
100 minutes of content creation
100 minutes of high-quality comments
100 minutes of outreach/DMs
= 5 hours/day, minimum 5 days/week
This is an advertising business model.
If you want more revenue, you must get more attention and direct it into your funnel.
⛓️ More attention → more conversations → more calls → more clients.
-----
🧠 How to Be Successful (The Speedrunner Mindset)
From Kirby’s Day 1 call recording:
There’s fun in the fast (speed = adventure + opportunities)
There’s fun in the grind (the reward is in the hustle)
You can always go faster than you think (you can do it)
There is fear in the fast — go anyway (become who you're capable of becoming)
If you want to crush this challenge:
Choose a goal that stretches you
Accept that fear is normal
Act before you feel ready
Let the scoreboard work for you instead of hiding from it
-----
✅ What You Should Do Now (Between Today and January 1)
If you aren’t willing to prepare properly, please do not join this challenge.
⭐️ By Friday, December 12th — Join & Orient

⭐️ By Wednesday, December 31st — Ready for Kickoff

If you show up on January 1st with these 12 steps done, you will be in an extremely strong position to make Q1 2026 your best quarter yet.

📣 What You’ll Get From Me

original_post.author: Jack Shiller
original_post.title: Why I quit youtube at 60,000 subscribers 😳
original_post.body:
A few years ago, I combined gaming highlights with my experience in video editing to create content on YouTube.
The content actually shifted more into teaching how to edit and create videos, and through my tutorials I grew to over 60,000 subscribers. I hosted calls with viewers, shared digital products, I even lived in a content house 🥲
And yet, dear synthesizers, daunting waves of doubt, fear, and confusion were stirring within me, becoming harder and harder to ignore... "Was I destined to live my whole life in a gaming niche? What did I truly want?”
Eventually, the burnout became undeniable as nothing lit me up and simple tasks felt exhausting. I was terribly confused and drowning in despair, and I have the voice memo diaries on my phone to prove it.
Even in the content house, I’d feel utterly depleted and alone…
So what did I do?
I broke up with my girlfriend at the time, moved to the middle of nowhere, left my channel entirely, and started my life over from scratch.
Silence, solitude, stillness… for two years.
Yes, at 21-years-old, I lived in the most simple and quiet lifestyle I have ever experienced. There were many days where I didn’t even say a single word.
I knew I still wanted to do something creative and entrepreneurial, but I didn’t have the slightest idea what it would be. I cold called businesses, tried to do copywriting services, I even drove to farms offering photography 🫠 none of it felt natural at all.
And you know what? I felt like I was going nowhere. No crazy ideas. No hit success.
On top of all of that, I pulled back from everyone and kept my work to myself… I was totally alone.
I realized that I could not keep doing what I was doing. Despite feeling like I was “trying,” I also felt like I was banging my head against the wall.
It was time for a different approach, to learn from @Andrew Kirby and @Aidan LaBreche...
Instead of an entrepreneur coming to people with solutions, it was time for me to come to others as a person who wants to understand their problems.
Instead of confining myself to what I’d done in the past, it was time to explore new avenues where I could truly be of service.
Instead of consuming content to find answers, it was time to contribute to communities to understand their questions.
That’s when Synthesizers comes in. I’ll let you finish the story 😉
🏓 Here is the full video, including pics and vids and what I'm doing now
💭 What changed for you since following the Synthesizer roadmap?

original_post.author: Sam Howe
original_post.title: Action → Relief → Confidence.
original_post.body:
I get overwhelmed when I'm carrying too much.
When something gets too heavy, everything slows down...
Then stops...
and then this pressure builds.
And when the pressure builds, everything feels heavier.
Fear kicks in.
And once fear kicks in, thinking becomes binary:
Good or bad.
Stay or run.
Survive or die.
Sounds dramatic, but in that headspace, it feels like there are only one or two options...
and both feel terrifying.
It's all driven by fear.
But today I remembered something I've learned before:
Action dissolves fear.
Not a big action, a tiny action.
But the problem is the big actions are the ones that show up in my mind:
"I need a new client."
"I need to move cities."
"I need to get fit."
"I need to make a whole film."
Of course that feels overwhelming.
Of course the pressure builds.
But it is ridiculous how just ten minutes of action can completely shift your state.
It's so ridiculous I often dismiss it as a real solution, but:
Ten minutes editing.
Ten minutes DMing.
Ten minutes exercising.
Ten minutes writing.
What happens next surprises me every time:
Relief.
And relief transforms into confidence.
Ten minutes turns into four, eight, ten hours of quality work.
My next film is about fear:
and how it's the biggest thing that holds us back.
Here are a few stills.
Can't wait to share it.
I hope if anyone is feeling that overwhelmed feeling I was this morning...
That this post helped and they can take ten minutes and get back after it.
Big love Synthesizers. XX
